title: Week 7 Guide
text:
In Weeks 5 and 6, you focused on Python’s code structure and assignment features: how programs are organized into statements and expressions, and how assignment forms like extended unpacking with * and the walrus operator (:=) go beyond what Gaddis covered. This week shifts from how code is structured to how Python handles decisions and repetition.
Chapters 12–13 revisit if statements and loops that you already know from CMSC 1203, but they explain design decisions behind these tools and introduce features Gaddis didn’t cover. This week has two main threads. The first is how Python evaluates truth. Not just True and False, but how every object carries its own truth value and how the Boolean operators and and or use that fact in ways that may surprise you. The second is how the for loop interacts with Python’s reference model from Weeks 3–4, where the same rules about rebinding and mutation determine whether a loop can actually change the data it iterates over.

title: Week 7 Guide
text:
Week 7 Demo

Here is the link to the Week 7 demo.
Download Jupyter Notebook

title: Week 7 Guide
text:
Chapters 12–13 concepts

Truth value testing and Boolean operators
In CMSC 1203, Boolean expressions produced True or False. Python’s truth value system is broader: every object has an inherent truth value, and the Boolean operators and and or return the operand object that determined the result, not a boolean. This enables common patterns for providing defaults and guarding against errors.
The demo examples show:

how bool() reveals truth values: zero and empty mean false, nonzero and nonempty mean true
why and and or return operand objects rather than True or False
short-circuit evaluation: default values with or, guarded access with and



The ternary expression
Week 5 introduced the distinction between statements and expressions. The ternary expression (Y if X else Z) brings selection into expression context, producing a value inline where an if statement cannot appear.
The demo examples show:

ternary syntax compared to multi-line if/else
why the ternary expression is preferred over the older and/or trick



Basic match/case
Python 3.10 introduced match/case as an alternative to long if/elif chains for matching a value against multiple options. The basic form handles simple value matching; the textbook covers advanced structural patterns that are beyond the scope of this course.
The demo examples show:

matching specific values and using _ as a wildcard default
combining multiple values in one case with |



Loop else and the search pattern
Python’s for and while loops support an else clause that runs only when the loop exits normally without hitting a break. This solves a specific problem: the search pattern, where you loop through a collection looking for an item and need to handle the not-found case without a flag variable.
The demo examples show:

how break skips the else clause
the search pattern: break when found, else handles not-found
eliminating flag variables



for loop mechanics and the reference model
This is the centerpiece of the week. The for loop assigns its variable through ordinary assignment on each iteration, the same rebinding operation from Weeks 3–4. This means modifying the loop variable does not change the list being iterated, a behavior that looks like a bug but follows directly from Python’s reference model.
The demo examples show:

why for x in L: x += 1 leaves L unchanged
how id() confirms the loop variable is rebound to a new object
connecting this behavior to the aliasing and rebinding concepts from Weeks 3–4



Modifying lists during iteration
Since the loop variable approach doesn’t modify the list, Python provides two alternatives. Index assignment with range(len()) mutates the list in place; list comprehensions build a new list. The choice depends on whether aliases to the original list should see the changes.
The demo examples show:

range(len()) with index assignment for in-place modification
list comprehensions for building a new list
when to use each approach (aliasing implications from Weeks 3–4)



Parallel traversal and enumeration
Chapters 12–13 introduce built-in functions that make for loops more expressive. zip() pairs items from multiple sequences for parallel traversal, and enumerate() provides index-and-item access without manual counters. Both produce tuples that can be unpacked directly in the for loop target, including extended unpacking with * from Week 5.
The demo examples show:

zip() for pairing sequences and dict(zip()) for building dictionaries
enumerate() for replacing range(len()) in read-only iteration
tuple unpacking in for targets with zip(), enumerate(), and dict.items()



Additional loop features in the reading
Chapters 12–13 cover several additional features not demonstrated this week. These are worth reading for awareness, though they are not the focus of the quiz.
The reading covers:

dictionary-based branching as an alternative to if/elif chains
advanced match/case structural patterns (beyond the basic form covered in the demo)
pass and ellipsis (...) as placeholder statements

title: Week 6 Guide
text:
Concepts being reinforced
This week’s work draws on the following concepts from Chapters 10–11:

Extended sequence unpacking
Week 5 introduced the * operator in unpacking assignments, which collects remaining elements into a list. This week you’ll use two different unpacking patterns to process data with variable-length structures: one where the variable portion is in the middle and one where it’s at the end. The position of * determines which elements get collected, and the starred name always produces a list regardless of how many elements it collects.


Named assignment expressions (walrus operator)
The walrus operator (:=) assigns a value to a name and returns that value as part of the expression. This week you’ll use := inside a loop to compute a value, test whether it meets a condition, and keep the result, all in a single expression without redundant computation. Pay attention to where the parentheses go: they control what gets assigned versus what gets compared.


Augmented assignment and mutation
This concept connects directly to the aliasing and mutation work from Weeks 3–4. When += is applied to a list, it mutates the list in place, so any other name referring to that list sees the change. When + is used instead, it creates a new list and rebinds the name, leaving other references unaffected. This week you’ll need to choose the right operator based on whether the original list should be preserved.

title: Week 5 Guide
text:
In Weeks 3 and 4, you focused on Python’s object model — how names refer to objects, how mutation differs from rebinding, and why aliasing produces bugs that silently give wrong results. This week shifts focus from what objects do to how Python organizes the code that works with them.
Chapters 10–11 of Learning Python explain Python’s design decisions about code structure. You already follow rules about indentation, colons, and assignment from CMSC 1203. These chapters explain why those rules exist, introduce the distinction between statements and expressions, and cover assignment features that go beyond what Gaddis covered — including extended sequence unpacking with *, the walrus operator (:=), and augmented assignment behavior with mutable objects.

Week 5 Demo

Here is the link to the Week 5 examples.
Download Quarto Notebook

title: Week 5 Guide
text:
Chapters 10–11 concepts

Program hierarchy
Python code is organized into a hierarchy: programs are composed of modules, modules contain statements, statements contain expressions, and expressions create and process objects. Understanding where code fits in this hierarchy helps you predict behavior and explains why certain syntax rules exist.
The demo examples show:

using type(), dir(), and id() to inspect modules and objects
tracing a single line of code through all four levels of the hierarchy
confirming that expressions evaluate to objects with types and identities



Statements vs expressions
Statements perform actions; expressions produce values. This distinction determines where code can and cannot appear — expressions can be used wherever a value is expected, but statements cannot.
The demo examples show:

expressions used in several contexts (assignment, function arguments, comprehensions)
what happens when a statement appears where an expression is expected
why this distinction motivates the walrus operator (covered below)



Indentation as syntax
Python uses indentation to define block structure rather than braces or keywords. The colon signals that an indented block follows, and IndentationError enforces consistency. This design means the code’s visual structure always matches its logical structure.
The demo examples show:

the colon-and-block pattern across if, for, and def
how changing indentation changes program behavior
how IndentationError prevents inconsistent blocks



Extended sequence unpacking
Basic unpacking from CMSC 1203 requires the number of targets to match the sequence length exactly. Extended unpacking uses * to collect remaining elements into a list, handling variable-length sequences cleanly.
The demo examples show:

the three common patterns: first, *rest / *init, last / first, *middle, last
what happens when the starred name collects zero elements
practical use with variable-length records



Line continuation and multiple statements
Chapter 10 covers how to write statements that span multiple lines and how to place multiple statements on one line. These are syntax details not covered in the demo but tested on the quiz.
The reading covers:

continuing a long statement with backslash or open brackets
placing multiple statements on one line with semicolons
why open brackets are the preferred continuation method



Augmented assignment and multi-target assignment
Chapter 11 covers assignment forms that connect directly to the aliasing and mutation concepts from Weeks 3–4.
The reading covers:

augmented operators (+=, -=, //=) and how += modifies a mutable object in place rather than creating a new one
multi-target assignment (a = b = c = 0) and the shared-reference behavior when the object is mutable



Named assignment expressions (walrus operator)
The walrus operator (:=) makes assignment an expression — it assigns a value to a name and returns that value so it can be used immediately. This fills the gap created by the statement/expression distinction.
The demo examples show:

basic := syntax compared to two-step alternatives
:= in if conditions, while loops, and comprehensions
why parentheses are needed around := in larger expressions



Expression statements and print parameters
An expression statement is an expression executed for its side effect rather than its return value — for example, calling my_list.sort() on its own line. Chapter 11 also covers the print() function’s keyword parameters (sep, end, file, flush) for controlling output formatting and destination.
The demo examples show:

custom separators and line endings with sep and end
redirecting output to a file with file
forcing immediate output with flush

The goal is to move from recognizing these concepts in isolation to identifying them when they cause real bugs in working code. This is a critical skill — these bugs don’t crash your program, they silently produce wrong results.

title: Week 4 Guide
text:
Week 4 is a reflection and consolidation week. There is no new reading this week. Instead, you will demonstrate your understanding of the Chapters 8–9 concepts by applying them to a practical debugging exercise.
The goal is to move from recognizing these concepts in isolation to identifying them when they cause real bugs in working code. This is a critical skill — these bugs don’t crash your program, they silently produce wrong results.

title: Week 4 Guide
text:
Concepts being reinforced
This week’s work draws on the following concepts from Chapters 8–9:

Aliasing and copying
When you assign a list to a new variable (b = a), both names refer to the same object. Changes through one name affect the other. To create an independent copy, you must explicitly copy the list.


Nested structures and the * operator
Using * to repeat a list containing mutable objects (like [[0]] * 3) creates multiple references to the same inner object, not independent copies. This causes unexpected behavior when you modify one “row.”


Shallow copy vs deep copy
A shallow copy (copy.copy()) creates a new outer object but shares nested objects with the original. A deep copy (copy.deepcopy()) recursively copies all nested objects, making them fully independent.


Mutable default arguments
When a mutable object (like a list) is used as a default parameter value, that object is created once at function definition time and shared across all calls. This causes values to accumulate unexpectedly.


Identity vs equality
The is operator tests whether two names refer to the same object (identity). The == operator tests whether two objects have the same value (equality). Using the wrong one produces incorrect comparisons.

title: Week 3 Guide
text:
This week is a refresher of Python’s built-in types. You already know how to make lists and dictionaries and loop over them. Chapters 8–9 extend that foundation by showing how to predict what happens when the same object is reused, when a type is mutated in place, and when data structures are nested.
In the Week 3 demo, the examples are designed to help you predict the behavior of Python’s built-in types. You will practice tracing what an operation does to an object (creates a new object vs changes an existing one), and you will learn how to explain the output you see in terms of identity, equality, and mutation.

title: Week 3 Guide
text:
Week 3 Demo

Here is the link to the Week 3 examples.
Download Jupyter Notebook

title: Week 3 Guide
text:
Chapters 8–9 concepts

Names, objects, and references
Chapters 8–9 review Python’s object model: variables are names that refer to objects, and collection objects store references to other objects rather than copying them. This explains why two names can refer to the same object, and why an in-place change can appear in more than one place.
The demo examples show:

identity vs equality (is vs ==)
how aliasing can occur through assignment
how mutation differs from rebinding for mutable objects



Sequence types (lists and tuples)
Chapter 8 presents lists and tuples as sequence types. Lists support in-place change, while tuples do not. These differences matter most when programs reuse values, build nested structures, or attempt to modify sequence items.
The demo examples show:

lists as mutable sequences (operations that change an existing list)
tuples as immutable sequences (item assignment fails)
slice assignment as an in-place list operation
correct and incorrect ways to construct nested lists



Mapping type (dictionaries)
Dictionaries are the built-in mapping type: values are stored and retrieved by key rather than by position. Chapter 8 covers dictionary creation and core operations used in real programs.
The demo examples show:

insertion order during iteration (order of insertion, not sorting)
safe access when a key may not exist (in, get)
controlled updates using copy and update
comparing dictionaries by comparing their key collections



Set types
Sets represent an unordered collection of unique values. Chapter 8 introduces sets and the operations used to test membership and reason about distinct values.
The demo examples show:

constructing sets to remove duplicates
membership testing with in
the hashability requirement for set elements (and why mutable types like lists cannot be elements)



Copying nested structures (shallow vs deep)
When a structure is nested (for example, a list of lists), “copy” can mean copying only the outer container or copying both outer and inner objects. Chapter 9 introduces this distinction and why it matters for program correctness.
The demo examples show:

shallow copy: new outer object with shared inner objects
deep copy: new outer object with copied inner objects



Course tool note: file access with pathlib.Path
The textbook uses open() as the standard and universal file interface. This week’s demo also introduces pathlib.Path, a modern alternative that is common in current Python code. pathlib is not covered in CMSC 1203 and it is not part of Chapters 8–9, but it builds directly on the same file concepts and simplifies common read/write tasks.

title: Using AI as a Learning Tool
text:
Purpose of this policy
Tools that use artificial intelligence are now widely available, and many of you already use them when learning technical material. This course allows the use of AI tools as a learning aid, similar to a tutor or reference source. At the same time, this course requires that submitted work reflect your own understanding and decision-making.
This page explains how AI tools may be used productively in this course, and where their use becomes inappropriate.


AI as a learning tool
Using AI as a learning tool means using it to clarify ideas, terminology, or examples that you do not yet understand.

Ideas An idea is a concept or principle discussed in the course materials, such as how a process works, why a particular approach is used, or what a tool is designed to do. Clarifying an idea means using AI to help you understand the purpose or reasoning behind something you have already encountered in class, rather than asking for a final answer.
Terminology Terminology refers to the specific words and phrases used in technical subjects. These terms often have precise meanings that differ from everyday usage. Using AI appropriately includes asking for explanations of unfamiliar terms so you can understand how they are used in context, especially when reading textbooks, documentation, or assignment instructions.
Examples Examples are demonstrations that show how an idea or concept is applied in practice. Using AI to clarify examples means asking for help understanding why an example works, what each part is doing, or how it relates to the underlying concept. It does not mean copying an example and submitting it as your own work.

In all cases, using AI as a learning tool should help you build understanding, not replace the effort of reading, experimenting, or reasoning through a problem yourself.


AI as a crutch
AI tools can support learning, but they can also be used in ways that reduce learning. This policy uses the term “crutch” to describe situations where the tool is doing the thinking and decision-making that the student is expected to practice.
Using AI as a crutch means relying on it to produce work or reasoning that you do not understand well enough to explain and defend. The issue is not that the tool was used; the issue is that the tool replaced the learning process.
Inappropriate uses include:

Asking AI to complete an assignment or lab task for you Example: “Write the solution for this assignment.”
Submitting AI-generated code, answers, or explanations that you cannot explain in your own words Example: Copying an explanation of why a solution works without being able to describe the steps yourself.
Treating AI output as authoritative without verification Example: Accepting an answer that conflicts with course materials, documentation, or results you can reproduce.
Using AI to bypass the learning objective Example: Asking for the final answer when the point of the activity is to practice reasoning, debugging, designing, or interpreting results.
Using AI to write or script reflections that are meant to describe your own understanding Example: Asking AI to generate a script or talking points for a reflection video instead of explaining the ideas in your own words.

In these cases, AI is being used to avoid the intended learning work rather than to support it.


Why this matters
AI can produce output that appears correct even when it contains errors or is based on incorrect assumptions. Because of that, it is important to treat AI output as a starting point for learning, not as an authority.
This course emphasizes:

verifying results,
understanding workflows,
and explaining decisions clearly.

If you rely on AI answers without testing them or understanding them, you are likely to miss errors and build misunderstandings. Those gaps tend to surface later in the course when tasks require independent problem solving, accurate interpretation, and clear explanations in graded work.


Expectations for submitted work
All submitted work must reflect your own understanding.
This means:

You should be able to explain any code you submit.
You should be able to describe why you made particular choices (model, metric, preprocessing).
In reflections, your explanations should sound like you, not like a generic answer generator.

You are not required to disclose routine AI use for clarification or studying. However, if your work suggests that you do not understand what you submitted, that will be treated the same way as any other lack of understanding, regardless of the source.
If you use an AI tool, ask yourself:

Could I explain this answer to another student without looking it up?
Did I test or verify what the tool suggested?
Did the tool help me understand, or did it just give me something to submit?

If the tool helped you understand, you are using it appropriately. If it replaced your thinking, you are not.

title: Week 8 Assignments
text:
Overview
This week is an application and consolidation week. You will demonstrate your understanding of Chapters 12–13 concepts by:

Writing a Python module from scratch that plugs into an existing program
Recording a video explanation of your code

Unlike Week 6 where you completed functions inside a provided file, this week you are creating a new file entirely. The main program imports your module and calls your functions. If the names, parameters, and return values match the spec below, the program will run and produce the correct output.

title: Week 8 Assignments
text:
Part 1: Module Creation Exercise

The Scenario
The development team behind Space Trader, a space trading simulation, needs a new module. The team lead has already written the main docking sequence program that handles display and program flow. Your job is to write the cargo module, which handles loading cargo, classifying it, scanning for contraband, applying tariffs, and generating a docking manifest.
The main program imports your module and calls each function with prepared data. You don’t modify the main program. You write the module it expects.


About the Program
The main program (space-trader.py) runs an automated docking sequence with no user input or menu system. When you run it, it executes all six sections and prints the results. With your module in place and working, each section will produce output that you can compare against the expected output at the end of this document.
The main program uses a few Python features in the display functions that you may not have written yourself yet. You don’t need to modify these functions, but here’s what they do:







Code
What It Does




f"{text:<22}"
Left-aligns text within 22 characters (used for table columns)


f"{text:^58}"
Centers text within 58 characters (used for headers)


f"{price:.2f}"
Formats a number with 2 decimal places (used for prices)


f"{rate:.0%}"
Formats a decimal as a percentage (e.g., 0.15 becomes 15%)


", ".join(list)
Combines list items into a single string with commas between them





Module Requirements
Your module file must be named lastname-cargo-module.py (replace lastname with your last name). It must contain one class and six functions.
Before you can run the program with your module, you need to update the import statement at the top of space-trader.py to match your filename. Change from cargo_module import to from lastname_cargo_module import (note: Python uses underscores in import statements, so the hyphens in your filename become underscores). This is the only change you should make to the main program.

Constraints

Use only concepts covered in CMSC 1203 and Weeks 1–7 of this course
Do not import any external libraries
Do not use try/except or error handling. Assume all inputs are valid
Each function should do one thing and return the specified result


1. CargoPod class
Write a class with __init__, __str__, and total_weight methods.
A CargoPod has six attributes: name, category, quantity, unit_weight, destination, and tags (a list of strings).
Look at the Cargo log section of the expected output to see what format __str__ should produce. Look at the Priority Unloading section to see total_weight() in action. It returns the quantity multiplied by the unit weight.
2. load_cargo(records)
Parse incoming cargo transmissions into CargoPod objects. Each record is a tuple, but the tuples vary in length. The first five elements are always (name, category, quantity, unit_weight, destination), and any remaining elements are optional tags.
Some transmissions are corrupted: they have an empty name or a zero quantity. Skip those records. Only create CargoPod instances for records with a truthy name and a truthy quantity.
Look at CARGO_RECORDS in the main program to see the tuple structures your code needs to handle. The variable-length tags are the same kind of pattern you saw with extended unpacking in the Week 5 demo.
Return a list of valid CargoPod instances.
3. classify_cargo(pods)
Assign handling instructions to each pod based on its category:

"weapons" → "Restricted - Secure Hold"
"food" → "Perishable - Cold Storage"
"tech" → "Fragile - Padded Bay"
"minerals" → "Heavy - Lower Deck"
anything else → "Standard Storage"

This is a value-matching problem. Review the match/case section of the Week 7 demo.
Return a list of (name, instruction) tuples.
4. scan_for_contraband(pods, banned_items)
Scan all cargo pods and report the first banned item found. If a pod’s name appears in the banned_items list, stop scanning immediately and return (True, "Contraband detected: {name}"). If no banned items are found after checking every pod, return (False, "Cargo scan clear").
The key behavior here is stopping at the first match and handling the not-found case separately. Review the loop else clause and search pattern from the Week 7 demo.
Return a tuple of (bool, str).
5. apply_tariffs(prices, rate, threshold)
Apply import tariffs to cargo prices by modifying the price list directly. For each price: if the price is greater than the threshold, replace it with the price multiplied by (1 + rate), rounded to 2 decimal places. If the price is at or below the threshold, leave it unchanged.
The main program passes a list and expects that same list to be modified. This is the in-place modification pattern using range(len()) with index assignment from the Week 7 demo. The conditional replacement is a natural fit for the ternary expression (Y if X else Z).
Return the modified list.
6. generate_manifest(names, prices, destinations)
Produce a numbered docking manifest from three parallel lists. Pair each name with its corresponding price and destination, and number the entries starting at 1.
This requires combining multiple sequences by position and adding an index. Review the zip(), enumerate(), and tuple unpacking sections of the Week 7 demo.
Return a list of (index, name, price, destination) tuples, 1-indexed.
7. filter_cargo_by_destination(pods, destination)
Build a new list containing only the pods whose destination matches the given destination string.
This is a filtering task that builds a new list from an existing one based on a condition. A list comprehension is the natural tool for this. Note how this contrasts with apply_tariffs, which modifies a list in place.
Return a new list of CargoPod instances.



Your Task

Download the main program using the link above
Create a new file named lastname-cargo-module.py (replace lastname with your last name)
Update the import statement in space-trader.py to match your module filename (hyphens become underscores)
Write the class and six functions described above
Run space-trader.py and verify your output matches the expected output at the end of this document



How to Approach This

Start with the CargoPod class. Several functions depend on it, and Section 1 will show you immediately whether your class works.
Read the main program. The display functions show you exactly what attribute names, method signatures, and return structures your code needs to produce.
Read the data. Look at CARGO_RECORDS to see how the tuples vary in length. This tells you what your unpacking needs to handle.
Test after each function. Run the program after completing each function to see your progress. Sections that depend on unfinished functions may produce errors, and that’s expected until those functions are in place.
Compare against the expected output. Each section has distinct, verifiable results.

title: Week 7 Assignments
text:
Part 1: Reading
Read Learning Python (6e), Chapters 12–13.
As you read, focus on these ideas (these are also the ideas the quiz will test):

Truth value testing: every object has a truth value; zero numbers, empty collections, None, and False are false; everything else is true
Boolean operators return objects: and and or return the operand that determined the result, not True or False
Short-circuit evaluation: or stops at the first true operand, and stops at the first false operand; the remaining expression is never evaluated
Ternary expression: Y if X else Z produces a value inline, unlike an if statement
match/case statement: selects a branch by matching a value against multiple cases, with _ as a wildcard default
Loop else clause: the else block on a for or while loop runs only when the loop completes without hitting break
for loop variable assignment: the loop variable is assigned via ordinary assignment each iteration; modifying it does not change the list
Modifying lists during iteration: index assignment with range(len()) mutates the list; list comprehensions build a new list
zip() for parallel traversal: pairs items from multiple sequences by position, truncating to the shortest
enumerate() for indexed iteration: produces (index, item) pairs, replacing manual counters and range(len()) for read-only access

title: Week 7 Assignments
section: Part 2: Work through the Week 7 Demo
text:
Part 2: Work through the Week 7 Demo
There is no video walkthrough for this week’s demo. Work through the examples carefully on your own – run each cell, read the explanations, and check whether you can predict the output before you see it.
The Week 7 demo covers:

Observing truth values with bool() and understanding how and and or return operand objects rather than booleans, including short-circuit evaluation patterns for defaults and guarded access
Writing a basic match/case statement for multiple-choice selection, including the _ wildcard default
Understanding the loop else clause, how break skips it, and using the search pattern to eliminate flag variables
Tracing why modifying a for loop variable does not change the list, connecting to the reference model from Weeks 3–4, and using range(len()) with index assignment or list comprehensions to actually modify values
Using zip() for parallel traversal, dict(zip()) for building dictionaries, and enumerate() for offset-and-item access
Unpacking tuples in for loop targets with zip(), enumerate(), and dict.items(), including extended unpacking with * (connecting to Week 5)

title: Week 7 Assignments
text:
Part 3: D2L Quiz
Complete the Week 7 D2L quiz using the quiz handout.

Download the Quiz Handout (PDF) using the link at the top of this page
Work through the 30 multiple-choice questions in the handout
Submit your answers (A, B, C, or D) in the D2L Quiz

The quiz focuses on:

predicting the truth value of Python objects using the rules for zero, empty, None, and False
predicting what and and or return when given non-Boolean operands
tracing short-circuit evaluation to determine which expressions are evaluated and which are skipped
predicting results of the ternary expression (Y if X else Z) and recognizing when the and/or trick fails
tracing a match/case statement to determine which branch executes
determining when a loop else clause runs and when break skips it
predicting whether a for loop that modifies its loop variable changes the list (connecting to the reference model)
distinguishing index assignment (L[i] = value) from loop variable rebinding and predicting when each modifies the list
predicting results of zip() with unequal-length sequences and dict(zip()) for dictionary construction
using enumerate() to predict (index, item) output and unpacking tuples in for loop targets

title: Week 6 Assignments
text:
Part 2: Code Completion Exercise

The Scenario
You’ve joined the development team at a small game studio working on Realm of Pytha, a fantasy RPG. The team has divided the game into modules, and you’ve been assigned to the party management module — the part of the game that handles recruiting characters, cataloging loot, checking carry weight, and managing inventories.
The previous developer on this module built the framework before moving to another project. The data, display functions, and main() are all in place and working. The core functions are defined but empty — they just have pass as a placeholder. Your job is to write the function bodies so the module produces the correct output.


About the Program
This program runs automatically — there’s no user input or menu system. When you run it, it executes all four sections and prints the results. With the placeholder functions in place, you’ll see messages like “No characters created” and “No results returned.” As you complete each function, its section will start producing real output.
The program uses a few Python features in the display functions that you may not have written yourself yet. You don’t need to modify these functions, but here’s what they do:







Code
What It Does




enumerate(items, 1)
Loops through a list while also tracking a counter that starts at 1


f"{text:<12}"
Left-aligns text within 12 characters (used for table columns)


", ".join(list)
Combines list items into a single string with commas between them





What to Complete
The program has five empty functions for you to complete. Each one requires a specific concept from Chapters 10–11.
1. Character class
Write the __init__ and __str__ methods. A character has a name, class, level, a list of skills, and a health value.
Look at display_party() to see what attribute names the display function expects.
The __str__ method should return a formatted string — look at the expected output to see what format main() produces when it prints each character.
2. parse_characters(records)
Each record in CHARACTER_RECORDS is a tuple where the first few elements are fixed, the last element is fixed, and the middle contains a variable number of skill names. Look at the data to see this pattern.
You need to separate the fixed parts from the variable-length middle. This is the first, *middle, last extended unpacking pattern from the Week 5 demo. Use the unpacked values to create Character instances and return them in a list.
3. parse_loot_drops(drops)
Each tuple in LOOT_DROPS has fixed elements at the beginning followed by a variable number of tags. Look at the data to see how the tuples vary in length.
This is a different unpacking pattern than function 2 — the variable-length portion is at the end. Review the extended unpacking patterns from the Week 5 demo. Return a list of dictionaries — look at display_loot() to see the expected keys.
4. find_heavy_items(weights, limit)
Loop through the weights dictionary and find items that exceed the limit. For each heavy item, compute how much it exceeds the limit by and include that in the result.
Use the walrus operator (:=) to compute the excess and check whether it’s greater than zero in a single expression. Review the walrus operator section of the Week 5 demo, and pay attention to where the parentheses go.
Look at display_heavy_items() to see what the display function expects.
5. combine_inventories(inv_a, inv_b)
Return a new list that combines both inventories. The key constraint is visible in the expected output: main() prints Aldric’s inventory after combining and expects it to be unchanged. Chapter 11 explains how += and + behave differently when applied to mutable objects like lists.


Your Task

Download the program using the link above
Rename the file to include your last name (e.g., smith-realm-of-pytha.py)
Run the program to see the current output with the placeholder functions
Complete each of the five functions described above
Run the completed program and verify your output matches the expected output at the end of this document



How to Approach This

Start with the class. Functions 2 and 3 depend on it, and you’ll be able to test Section 1 immediately.
Read the display functions. They show you exactly what attribute names and dictionary keys your code needs to produce.
Read the data. Look at CHARACTER_RECORDS and LOOT_DROPS to see the tuple structures your unpacking needs to handle.
Test after each function. Run the program after completing each function to see your progress.

title: Week 5 Assignments
text:
Part 1: Reading
Read Learning Python (6e), Chapters 10–11.
As you read, focus on these ideas (these are also the ideas the quiz will test):

Program hierarchy: programs are composed of modules, modules contain statements, statements contain expressions, and expressions create and process objects
Statements vs expressions: statements perform actions; expressions produce values — and each can appear only in certain places
Indentation as syntax: Python uses indentation to define blocks, the colon signals the start of an indented block, and inconsistent indentation causes an IndentationError
Line continuation: how to continue a long statement across lines using backslash or open brackets, and how to place multiple statements on one line with semicolons
Extended sequence unpacking: the * operator collects remaining elements into a list during unpacking (e.g., first, *rest = [1, 2, 3, 4])
Augmented assignment: operators like +=, -=, and //=, and how += can modify a mutable object in place rather than creating a new one
Multi-target assignment: a = b = c = 0 creates a shared reference, and mutations through one name affect all names
Named assignment expressions (walrus operator): := assigns a value and returns it in a single expression, useful in conditions and loops
Expression statements: expressions like my_list.sort() can stand alone as statements when called for their side effects
Print parameters: sep, end, file, and flush control how print() formats and delivers output

Using extended sequence unpacking (*) to capture variable-length portions of sequences in patterns like first, *rest, *init, last, and first, *middle, last
Using the walrus operator (:=) to assign and test a value in a single expression, including use in if conditions, while loops, and comprehensions
Controlling print() output with sep, end, file, and flush parameters

title: Week 4 Quiz – Debugging Python’s Object Model
text:
Question 1
Consider this code:
def add_pet(name, pets=[]):
    pets.append(name)
    return pets

print(add_pet('Buddy'))
print(add_pet('Luna'))
What is the complete output (both lines)?
A. ['Buddy'] then ['Luna']
B. Error: list index out of range
C. ['Buddy'] then ['Buddy', 'Luna']
D. ['Buddy', 'Luna'] then ['Buddy', 'Luna']

title: Week 4 Quiz – Debugging Python’s Object Model
text:
Question 2
Consider this code:
morning = ['Buddy', 'Luna']
afternoon = morning
afternoon.append('Charlie')
What does morning contain?
A. ['Buddy', 'Luna']
B. ['Charlie']
C. ['Buddy', 'Luna', 'Luna', 'Charlie']
D. ['Buddy', 'Luna', 'Charlie']

title: Week 4 Quiz – Debugging Python’s Object Model
text:
Question 3
A programmer writes:
schedule = [[6, 6, 6]] * 7
schedule[2][2] = 10
They expected only Wednesday’s evening slot to change. What actually happens?
A. The evening slot (index 2) of ALL seven days becomes 10
B. Only schedule[2][2] becomes 10
C. The entire schedule becomes 10
D. Python raises an IndexError

title: Week 4 Quiz – Debugging Python’s Object Model
text:
Question 4
Consider this code:
import copy
nested = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
backup = copy.copy(nested)
backup[0].append(99)
What is nested[0]?
A. [1, 2]
B. [3, 4]
C. [99, 1, 2]
D. [1, 2, 99]

title: Week 4 Quiz – Debugging Python’s Object Model
text:
Question 5
Consider this code:
pet_a = {'name': 'Buddy', 'age': 3}
pet_b = {'name': 'Buddy', 'age': 3}
What is the result of pet_a is pet_b?
A. True
B. False
C. Error: cannot compare dictionaries
D. None

title: Week 4 Quiz – Debugging Python’s Object Model
text:
Question 6
A programmer wants to copy a list without aliasing. The buggy code is:
event_pets = shelter_pets
Which replacement correctly creates an independent copy?
A. event_pets = shelter_pets
B. event_pets = shelter_pets is shelter_pets
C. event_pets = shelter_pets.copy()
D. event_pets == shelter_pets

title: Week 4 Quiz – Debugging Python’s Object Model
Week 4 Quiz – Debugging Python’s Object Model
text:
Question 8
A function checks if two pets are the same using:
if pet1 is pet2:
    return True
return False
Two Pet objects have identical name, species, and age, but the function returns False. What is wrong?
A. The function should compare pet1.name only
B. Nothing is wrong; the function is correct
C. The function should use if pet1 = pet2
D. The function should use == instead of is to compare values

title: Week 4 Quiz – Debugging Python’s Object Model
text:
Question 9
Consider this code:
import copy
original = {'name': 'Buddy', 'stats': [5, 5, 5]}
backup = copy.copy(original)
backup['stats'][0] = 10
What is original['stats']?
A. [5, 5, 5]
B. [10, 5, 5]
C. [10, 10, 10]
D. None

title: Week 4 Quiz – Debugging Python’s Object Model
text:
Question 10
Consider this code:
def register(pet, history=[]):
    history.append(pet)
    return history

register('Buddy')
register('Luna')
register('Max')
result = register('Bella')
print(len(result))
What is printed?
A. 4
B. 1
C. 2
D. 3

title: Week 4 Quiz – Debugging Python’s Object Model
text:
Question 11
A function has a mutable default argument bug:
def add_note(text, notes=[]):
    notes.append(text)
    return notes
Which fix is correct?
A. Change notes=[] to notes={}
B. Remove the notes parameter entirely
C. Change notes=[] to notes=None, then add if notes is None: notes = [] inside the function
D. Change notes=[] to notes=()

title: Week 4 Quiz – Debugging Python’s Object Model
text:
Question 12
Consider this code:
import copy
original = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
backup = copy.deepcopy(original)
backup[0].append(99)
What is original?
A. [[1, 2, 99], [3, 4]]
B. [[1, 2], [3, 4, 99]]
C. [[1, 2, 99], [3, 4, 99]]
D. [[1, 2], [3, 4]]

title: Week 4 Quiz – Debugging Python’s Object Model
text:
Question 13
Which code creates a 3x3 grid where each row is an independent list?
A. grid = [[0, 0, 0]] * 3
B. grid = [[0, 0, 0] for _ in range(3)]
C. row = [0, 0, 0]; grid = [row, row, row]
D. grid = [0, 0, 0] * 3

y = x

title: Week 4 Assignments
text:
Part 2: Debugging Exercise

The Scenario
You’ve been hired as a junior developer at Sunshine Animal Shelter. On your first day, several employees come to you with complaints about the shelter management program:
Maria (Event Coordinator): > “I created a separate list for Saturday’s adoption event and added a new dog named Charlie to it. But now Charlie is showing up on the main shelter roster too! I didn’t add him there. The lists are supposed to be separate.”
James (Feeding Staff): > “I updated Wednesday’s evening feeding to 10 portions for a special diet. But now EVERY day shows 10 portions in the evening! I only changed Wednesday!”
Dr. Patel (Veterinarian): > “Before transferring Whiskers to a partner shelter, I made a backup of her profile. Then I added a transfer exam note to the backup. But somehow that note appeared on her original record too! The backup was supposed to be independent.”
Sarah (Adoption Specialist): > “I processed three adoptions today — Hoppy, Max, and Luna. But when I looked at the records, they all have each other’s information mixed together! Hoppy’s record shows Max’s adopter name, and Luna’s record has everyone’s notes. Each adoption should have its own separate paperwork.”
Tom (Database Admin): > “We have two entries for a dog named Bella — same name, same breed, same age. The duplicate checker says they’re different pets, but they’re clearly the same dog entered twice. Why isn’t it catching this?”
Your supervisor says: > “The weird thing is, the program runs fine — no error messages. But the output is clearly wrong. These are all experienced employees; they know how the system should work. Can you figure out what’s going on in the code?”


About the Program
This program simulates a full day of shelter operations automatically — there’s no user input or menu system. When you run it, it immediately executes all six sections and prints the results. This is by design, so you can focus on reading the output and finding bugs rather than navigating menus.
The program is also longer than what you’ve written so far, and it uses a few Python features we haven’t covered yet. These features are only used in the display/helper functions — not in the buggy functions you need to fix. You don’t need to fully understand them to complete this assignment, but here’s what they do:







Code
What It Does




enumerate(pets, 1)
Loops through a list while also tracking a counter that starts at 1 (used for numbered lists)


f"{text:^50}"
Centers text within 50 characters (used for headers)


f"{text:<12}"
Left-aligns text within 12 characters (used for table columns)



Focus on the five short functions where the bugs live — each is only 2–5 lines of straightforward code using concepts from Chapters 8–9. The five functions you need to debug are:

copy_available_pets()
create_feeding_schedule()
backup_pet_profile()
register_adoption()
find_duplicate_profiles()

The rest of the program (display functions, sample data setup, the Pet class, main()) is scaffolding that just works.
Important: Your fixes should only involve changing code within these five functions. Do not add new functions, delete existing functions, or modify the main() automation. Each bug can be fixed by changing 1–2 lines in its function.


Your Task
The program contains 5 bugs — one causing each employee’s problem. Each bug relates to a concept from Chapters 8–9.

Download the program using the link above
Rename the file to include your last name (smith-pet-shelter.py)
Run the program and observe the output
Investigate each employee’s complaint by finding the responsible function
For each bug:

Identify the function and line where the bug occurs
Explain the concept being violated
Fix the code

Run the corrected program to verify you’ve resolved all five complaints



How to Find the Bugs

Read the output carefully. Compare what you see to each employee’s complaint.
Trace the code. When you see unexpected output, find the function responsible and read it line by line.
Think about Chapters 8–9. Each bug involves one of these concepts: copying lists, creating nested lists, copying objects with nested data, function parameters, or comparing objects.

title: Week 3 Assignments
text:
Part 1: Reading
Read Learning Python (6e), Chapters 8–9.
As you read, focus on these ideas (these are also the ideas the quiz will test):

Names and objects: variables are names that refer to objects
Identity vs equality: what is tests vs what == tests
Aliasing: how two names can refer to the same object through assignment
Mutability: what it means for an object (such as a list or dictionary) to be mutable
Rebinding vs mutation (lists): the difference between assigning a name to a new list and changing an existing list in place
List slicing and slice assignment: what a slice means and how assigning to a slice changes a list
Nested list construction: why * repetition can create repeated references when building a list of lists
Dictionaries as mappings: key-based access, insertion order during iteration, and update patterns
Safe dictionary access: membership tests with in and default-returning access with get
Sets: uniqueness, membership testing, and the hashability requirement for set elements
Tuples: indexing like other sequences and immutability (item assignment fails)
Copying nested structures: what shallow copy and deep copy mean for nested lists

identifying controlled dictionary update patterns using copy and update
using sets for uniqueness and explaining why unhashable types (such as lists) cannot be set elements
identifying tuple behaviors: indexing works, but item assignment fails

title: CMSC 1236 – Advanced Programming in Python
text:
Develop a Python program to simulate a virtual pet. This project will allow you to apply and demonstrate your understanding of Python basics, object-oriented programming, file handling using pickling, and user interaction through the console.



The Pet class is a core component of your Virtual Pet Simulator. It will model the behavior and state of your virtual pet. You will need to use conditional statements (if statements) in the methods of this class to simulate different scenarios based on the pet’s attributes.

Implementing the Pet Class:

Follow the provided UML diagram to create the Pet class with the attributes name, hunger, happiness, energy, color, and type.
Initialize these attributes in the __init__ method. name, color, and type will be strings provided when a new pet is created, and hunger, happiness, and energy should start with random values between 0 and 10.

Using Conditional Statements in Class Methods:

In methods like feed, play, and rest, you will use if statements to check the state of the pet and decide what action to take.









The feed method is designed to simulate feeding the pet. Feeding the pet has implications on its hunger and happiness levels.




Reducing Hunger and Increasing Happiness:

When the feed method is called, first check the pet’s current hunger level (self.hunger).
If self.hunger is greater than 0, it indicates that the pet is hungry. In this case, reduce self.hunger by 1 to represent feeding the pet. Ensure self.hunger does not go below 0.
After feeding, increase self.happiness by 1. However, ensure that self.happiness does not exceed 10.

Managing Happiness When Not Hungry:

If self.hunger is already at 0 (meaning the pet is not hungry), feeding the pet doesn’t reduce hunger will affect its happiness. Reduce self.happiness by 1, since overfeeding will cause discomfort.

Returning Feedback:

Return a feedback string that reflects the pet’s reaction to being fed.








The play method simulates the interaction of playing with the pet. This activity affects the pet’s happiness and energy levels.




Handling Sadness:

Before initiating play, check if the pet’s happiness level (self.happiness) is at 0.
If self.happiness is 0, it indicates that the pet is too sad to engage in play.

Handling Tiredness:

Check the pet’s energy level (self.energy).
If self.energy is 0, it means the pet is too tired to play.

Playing with the Pet:

If the pet is neither too sad nor too tired (i.e., self.happiness is above 0 and self.energy is above 0), proceed to play with the pet.
Increase self.happiness by 1 to represent the positive effect of playing. However, ensure that self.happiness does not exceed 10.
Decrease self.energy by 1 to account for the energy expended during play. However, it’s important to ensure that self.energy does not fall below 0.

Returning Feedback:

Return a feedback string that reflects the pet’s reaction to playing.








The rest method is designed to simulate the pet resting, which affects its energy and happiness levels. Resting is essential for the pet to regain energy, but it might also affect its happiness if it becomes bored or inactive.




Increasing Energy:

Check the pet’s current energy level (self.energy).
If self.energy is less than 10, it indicates the pet could benefit from rest. Increase self.energy by 1 to represent the energy regained from resting. However, ensure that self.energy does not exceed 10.

Managing Happiness When Energy is at Maximum:

If self.energy is already at 10 (meaning the pet is fully rested), resting will affect its happiness.
If self.energy is already at 10 and self.happiness is greater than 0, reduce self.happiness it by 1 to represent a slight decrease in happiness due to inactivity or boredom. Be sure to prevent self.happiness from going below 0.

Returning Feedback:

Return a feedback string that reflects the pet’s reaction to resting.








The customize method allows the user to change the pet’s appearance by setting new values for the color and type attributes.




Setting New Attributes:

The method takes two parameters, color and pet_type, and assigns them to the pet’s color and type attributes.
This allows the user to update the pet’s appearance during the simulation. Display the changed output to the user.








The status method provides a summary of the current state of the animal, including its hunger, happiness, energy, color, and type. This method is crucial for understanding the animal’s overall well-being at any given moment.




Creating a Status Summary:

The method compiles a string that summarizes the animal’s current status.
This summary includes the animal’s name and its current hunger, happiness, energy, color, and type.

String Formatting:

Create a readable and well-formatted summary.
Each attribute (hunger, happiness, energy, color, type) should be on a separate line for clarity.

Returning the Summary:

The method returns the formatted string.








The is_happy method is designed to evaluate the animal’s happiness level and determine if the animal is currently happy. This method provides a boolean result based on the happiness attribute.




Evaluating Happiness:

The method assesses whether the animal’s happiness level (self.happiness) is below or above 5.
If self.happiness is above 5 the animal is considered to be happy.

Returning a Boolean Result:

The method returns True if the animal’s happiness is above 5, indicating that the animal is happy.
It returns False if the happiness level is 5 or lower, indicating that the animal is not happy.








Your main() function serves as the interaction hub between the user and their virtual pet. It will manage user inputs through a menu system and trigger appropriate actions based on the user’s choices.




Implement a menu system that displays a list of possible actions the user can perform. These actions include:

Feeding the pet
Playing with the pet
Letting the pet rest
Checking the pet’s status
Customizing the pet’s appearance
Saving the pet’s state and exiting the program

Use an input prompt to allow the user to select an action and then call the corresponding method from the Pet class.





Ensure that the program gracefully handles invalid menu choices. (This needs to be demonstrated in submission video).
Display an error message if the user inputs an invalid option and show the menu again for a valid selection.






Use pickling to save and load the pet’s state. Implement a save_pet and a load_pet function in the main() for this purpose.
Ensure the program can handle situations where no saved data exists.

title: CMSC 1236 – Python Environment Setup (Windows)
text: For CMSC 1236, we will create a separate Python environment so nothing conflicts with your other classes.

title: CMSC 1236 – Python Environment Setup (Windows)
section: Step 2 — Create your course folder on the root drive
text:
Step 2 — Create your course folder on the root drive
Your CMSC 1236 directory must be created on the root of the C: drive:

C:\cmsc-1236

Inside it, create these folders:

assignments
notes
projects

Creating the course folder on the root drive helps avoid common Windows problems:

Short, predictable paths (fewer issues with tools that don’t like long file paths).
Avoids OneDrive syncing (Desktop/Documents are often synced; syncing can lock files and break builds/tools).
Makes Git + terminals simpler (easy to navigate, fewer permission surprises).
Keeps course work separate from other software setups (so you don’t accidentally mix files/tools between courses).


CMD commands (Command Prompt)

Open Command Prompt (cmd).
Go to the root of the C: drive:

cd\

Create the course folder:

mkdir cmsc-1236

Move into the course folder:

cd \cmsc-1236

Create the three required subfolders:

mkdir assignments
mkdir notes
mkdir projects


Verify the folder structure
Run:
dir
Your folder structure should look like similar to the following:
C:\cmsc-1236\
  assignments\
  notes\
  projects\

title: CMSC 1236 – Python Environment Setup (Windows)
section: Step 3 — Create the CMSC 1236 Python environment
text:
Step 3 — Create the CMSC 1236 Python environment
We will create a dedicated environment named:

cmsc1236

This environment is separate from any miniconda environments you may be using in other courses, so nothing conflicts.
A dedicated environment ensures that we:

Keep CMSC 1236 packages separate from other classes/tools
Ensure the course runs on a consistent Python version


Create the environment (Command Prompt)

Open Command Prompt (cmd)

Go to your course folder (if not already there):

cd \cmsc-1236

Create the environment folder using Python’s built-in venv tool:

py -m venv cmsc1236


Activate the environment (Command Prompt)
Run:
cmsc1236\Scripts\activate
When it activates, your prompt will look like this:
(cmsc1236) C:\cmsc-1236>


Verify the environment is working
Run:
python --version
You should see a Python version printed:
Python 3.xx.x


Deactivate the environment
When you are finished:
deactivate

title: CMSC 1236 – Python Environment Setup (Windows)
section: Step 4 — Initialize Git in your course folder
text:
Step 4 — Initialize Git in your course folder
We use Git to track your work over time and to make submitting or backing up your work easier.
This step creates a .git folder that stores your work history.

Initialize the repository
Open Command Prompt (cmd) and run:
cd C:\cmsc-1236
git init


Create a .gitignore file
Git does not create a .gitignore automatically. You will create it so that you don’t accidentally commit:

Python cache files
editor settings
temporary files

In C:\cmsc-1236, create a file named:
.gitignore
Paste the following into it:
# Python cache
__pycache__/
*.pyc

# Environment / OS clutter
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

# VS Code
.vscode/

# Local environment folder 
cmsc1236/

# Common local files
*.log
*.tmp

title: CMSC 1236 – Python Environment Setup (Windows)
section: Step 5 — Set VS Code to use the CMSC 1236 environment
text:
Step 5 — Set VS Code to use the CMSC 1236 environment
In this step, you will tell VS Code which Python interpreter to use for this course so that:

Run/Debug uses the correct Python
The integrated terminal runs the correct Python
Your assignments behave consistently across machines


Note: This environment is separate from the Miniconda environments you may be using in other courses, so nothing conflicts.


Open your course folder in VS Code


VS Code stores interpreter settings per folder/project
Opening the correct folder ensures the interpreter you select is tied to CMSC 1236 only


Select the correct Python interpreter



Select View → Command Palette or Press: Ctrl + Shift + P

Type: Python: Select Interpreter
Choose the interpreter that points to the course environment:

C:\cmsc-1236\cmsc1236\Scripts\python.exe
If you do not see it:

Choose Enter interpreter path…
Then browse to: C:\cmsc-1236\cmsc1236\Scripts\python.exe


Verify in the VS Code Terminal (Command Prompt)



In VS Code, open a CMD Prompt terminal:

Terminal → New Terminal

Confirm you are in the course folder. If not, run:

cd C:\cmsc-1236

Activate the course environment:

cmsc1236\Scripts\activate

Verify that Python is coming from the course environment:

where python
Expected output includes a path like:
C:\cmsc-1236\cmsc1236\Scripts\python.exe

Confirm the Python version:

python --version

If where python does NOT point into C:\cmsc-1236\cmsc1236\..., you are not using the course environment
Fixing it here prevents “it works on my machine” problems later

Step 7 — Import the book example code
You’ll keep the books’ example code in your course folder so you can quickly reference working examples while reading and while completing assignments.

cd C:\cmsc-1236
mkdir book
cd book

Fluent Python (2nd Edition) — clone the official GitHub repo

git clone https://github.com/fluentpython/example-code-2e.git
This creates:
C:\cmsc-1236\book\fluent-python-2e\


Learning Python (6th Edition) — download the official examples ZIP
Learning Python’s official examples are provided as a ZIP download from the book’s site (not a GitHub repo).

Open this page in your browser: https://learning-python.com/LP6E-code.html
Click LP6E.zip ⇦ Fetch and unzip and download it.



Unzip it

Right-click the ZIP → Extract All…
Extract into: C:\cmsc-1236\book\

After unzipping, you should see an LP6E\ folder in C:\cmsc-1236\book\.

title: CMSC 1236 — Week 3 Demo
text:
CMSC 1203 introduced lists, dictionaries, and sets with an emphasis on correct use: create a data structure, loop over it, and produce correct output. Chapters 8–9 build on that foundation by explaining how Python’s built-in types behave at runtime.
In this notebook, the term collection type refers to a built-in type that groups multiple values into a single object (for example: list, dict, set, and tuple). The goal is to understand how these types store and share references, and how operations on them affect program behavior as code and data structures become larger.
After completing these examples, you should be able to:

explain the difference between identity and equality (is vs ==)
distinguish rebinding from in-place mutation and predict when aliasing causes a change to appear in more than one place
use list operations shown in the reading (including slice assignment and nested list construction) and predict when they modify an existing list
describe key dictionary behaviors emphasized in the reading: insertion order, safe key access, and controlled updates
use sets for uniqueness and understand why set elements must be hashable
explain why tuples are used for fixed groupings and why item assignment is not allowed
copy nested structures intentionally by choosing between a shallow copy and a deep copy
read and write a text file using pathlib.Path (read_text() and write_text())

In CMSC 1203, you learned how to use Python’s built-in collections. You practiced calling list methods like append() and pop(), writing list comprehensions, using dictionary methods like get() and update(), and reading and writing files with open().
This unit shifts focus to how Python works internally. The goal is to understand what happens when you assign one variable to another, why a change to one list sometimes affects another variable, and when copying a data structure actually matters. These concepts explain behavior that may have seemed unexpected in CMSC 1203 and will help you predict and debug your code as programs become larger and more complex.
