title: CMSC 1236 — Week 3 Demo
text:
Part 1: Names, objects, and references
In Python, a variable is a name that refers to an object. An object is a value stored in memory (for example, a list, a dictionary, an integer, or a string). When a name refers to an object, the name is said to be bound to that object.
This model is a basic part of programming in Python. It explains how changes to lists and dictionaries propagate through a program, and it helps you predict what happens when the same object is reused in more than one place. It also applies when a value is passed into a function, meaning the object is provided to a function as an argument so the function can use it. If the argument is a mutable object (an object whose contents can be changed after it is created) and the function mutates it, the change is visible after the function call because the same object is being used.
Python’s built-in collection types (lists, dictionaries, sets, and tuples) store references to objects rather than copying them. As a result, the same object can be reached through more than one name.
Two operations are used throughout Python programs, and they are easy to confuse because both can occur after an assignment statement. The difference is what changes:

rebinding changes which object a name refers to; the original object is unchanged
mutation changes the contents of an existing object; any name that refers to that object will observe the change

Because Python programs frequently reuse objects through multiple names (intentionally or unintentionally), it is important to distinguish “same object” from “same value” so you can predict when a change will affect more than one variable. Two comparison operators are used for that purpose:

is tests identity: whether two names refer to the same object in memory
== tests equality: whether two objects have the same value

The following two examples establish the difference between identity and equality for collection objects.

Example 1: Two names referring to the same object
This first example shows what it means for two names to refer to the same object. The output will confirm that a is b is true (same object) and a == b is also true (same contents).

a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a
print("a =", a)
print("b =", b)
print("a is b =", a is b)
print("a == b =", a == b)

a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [1, 2, 3]
a is b = True
a == b = True


Explanation:

a = [1, 2, 3] creates a list object containing the integers 1, 2, and 3, and assigns the name a to refer to that list.
b = a assigns the name b to refer to the same list object as a. No new list is created.
print("a =", a) prints the label a = followed by the current value of a (the list object that a refers to).
print("b =", b) prints the label b = followed by the current value of b (the same list object, since b refers to the same object as a).
print("a is b =", a is b) prints the label a is b = followed by the result of the identity test a is b. This is True here because a and b refer to the same object in memory.
print("a == b =", a == b) prints the label a == b = followed by the result of the equality test a == b. This is True here because the two values have the same contents (and in this case they are the same list object).



Example 2: Different objects with the same value
This example shows that two different list objects can still be equal in value. The key observation is that x is y is false because two separate list objects are created, while x == y is true because the contents of the two lists match.

x = [1, 2]
y = [1, 2]

print("x =", x)
print("y =", y)
print("x is y =", x is y)
print("x == y =", x == y)

x = [1, 2]
y = [1, 2]
x is y = False
x == y = True


Explanation:

x = [1, 2] creates a list object containing the integers 1 and 2, and assigns the name x to refer to that list.
y = [1, 2] creates a second list object containing the integers 1 and 2, and assigns the name y to refer to that second list. Even though the contents match, this is a different object in memory from the one referenced by x.
print("x =", x) prints the label x = followed by the current value of x (the list object that x refers to).
print("y =", y) prints the label y = followed by the current value of y (the list object that y refers to).
print("x is y =", x is y) prints the label x is y = followed by the result of the identity test x is y. This is False because x and y refer to different list objects in memory.
print("x == y =", x == y) prints the label x == y = followed by the result of the equality test x == y. This is True because the two lists contain the same values in the same order.



Example 3: Truth values of objects
In Python, every object has an inherent truth value, which means any object can be used directly in a condition such as an if statement. This matters in practice because checking whether a collection is empty is one of the most common operations in everyday code. Instead of writing if len(items) > 0: to test whether a list has elements, Python programmers write if items: and rely on the object’s truth value. Understanding which objects are considered true and which are considered false helps you read and write idiomatic Python code.
The general rule is: - False: zero values (0, 0.0), empty collections ("", [], {}, (), set()), and None - True: everything else (non-zero numbers, non-empty collections)

empty_list = []
non_empty_list = [0, 0, 0]

print("empty_list =", empty_list)
print("non_empty_list =", non_empty_list)
print()

if empty_list:
    print("empty_list is truthy")
else:
    print("empty_list is falsy")

if non_empty_list:
    print("non_empty_list is truthy")
else:
    print("non_empty_list is falsy")

empty_list = []
non_empty_list = [0, 0, 0]

empty_list is falsy
non_empty_list is truthy


Explanation:

empty_list = [] creates an empty list and assigns empty_list to refer to it.
non_empty_list = [0, 0, 0] creates a list containing three zeros and assigns non_empty_list to refer to it. The list is non-empty even though all its elements are zero.
The first two print statements display both lists so their contents are visible.
if empty_list: tests the truth value of empty_list. Because the list is empty, it is considered false, so the else branch executes.
if non_empty_list: tests the truth value of non_empty_list. Because the list contains items (even though those items are zeros), it is considered true, so the if branch executes.

title: CMSC 1236 — Week 3 Demo
text:
Part 2: Rebinding vs in-place mutation (lists)
Lists are mutable sequence objects, which means their contents can be changed after they are created. When lists are used in a program, two different kinds of changes are common:

a name can be reassigned to refer to a different list (rebinding)
an existing list can be changed directly (in-place mutation)

This distinction matters because it affects whether a change is visible in other parts of a program. If two names refer to the same list object, then an in-place change is observed through both names. If a name is rebound to a new list object, other names that still refer to the original list are unaffected.
The following examples use two names (nums and alias) that initially refer to the same list so that the difference between rebinding and mutation is visible in the output.

Example 1: Rebinding creates a new list
In this example, nums = nums + [40] produces a new list object and assigns nums to refer to that new list. The name alias continues to refer to the original list.

nums = [10, 20, 30]
alias = nums

nums = nums + [40]

print("nums =", nums)
print("alias =", alias)
print("nums is alias =", nums is alias)

nums = [10, 20, 30, 40]
alias = [10, 20, 30]
nums is alias = False


Explanation:

nums = [10, 20, 30] creates a list object containing 10, 20, and 30, and assigns the name nums to refer to it.
alias = nums assigns the name alias to refer to the same list object as nums. No new list is created.
nums = nums + [40] uses + to create a new list that contains the items from the original nums list followed by 40. After the new list is created, nums is rebound so it refers to the new list object. The original list object is not changed in place, so alias still refers to the original list.
print("nums =", nums) prints the new list that nums now refers to.
print("alias =", alias) prints the original list that alias still refers to.
print("nums is alias =", nums is alias) prints whether nums and alias refer to the same list object. This is False because nums was rebound to a different list.



Example 2: In-place mutation changes the existing list
In this example, nums += [40] changes the existing list object in place. Because alias refers to the same list object, alias reflects the change as well.

nums = [10, 20, 30]
alias = nums

nums += [40]

print("nums =", nums)
print("alias =", alias)
print("nums is alias =", nums is alias)

nums = [10, 20, 30, 40]
alias = [10, 20, 30, 40]
nums is alias = True


Explanation:

nums = [10, 20, 30] creates a list object containing 10, 20, and 30, and assigns the name nums to refer to it.
alias = nums assigns the name alias to refer to the same list object as nums. No new list is created.
nums += [40] modifies the existing list object in place by adding 40 to the end. The name nums still refers to the same list object as before.
print("nums =", nums) prints the modified list.
print("alias =", alias) prints the same modified list, because alias refers to the same object.
print("nums is alias =", nums is alias) prints whether nums and alias refer to the same list object. This is True because the list was mutated in place rather than replaced.

title: CMSC 1236 — Week 3 Demo
section: Part 3: Lists (sequence type): slice assignment and nested list construction
text:
Part 3: Lists (sequence type): slice assignment and nested list construction
This section focuses on two list operations that are easy to use correctly in small examples, but can cause confusion in larger programs if the effect on the existing list object is not understood. First, lists support slice assignment, which replaces part of a list by assigning to a slice. A slice is a range of positions written as start:stop (the stop position is not included). This matters because slice assignment changes the existing list in place rather than creating a new list, so the change is visible anywhere that same list is referenced.
Second, lists support repetition with *. When the repeated item is itself a mutable object (such as a list), repetition can produce a nested structure in which multiple positions refer to the same inner object. This matters because changing one inner list then appears to change multiple “rows” at once.
The examples in this section demonstrate slice assignment as an in-place change, and they contrast two ways to build a list of lists: one that repeats references and one that creates independent inner lists.

Example 1: Slice assignment modifies a list in place
Slice assignment replaces part of a list without creating a new list object. The key observation is that the list referenced by data changes, but data continues to refer to the same list object.

data = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

print("before:", data)

data[2:5] = ["a", "b"]

print("after: ", data)

before: [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
after:  [0, 1, 'a', 'b', 5]


Explanation:

data = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5] creates a list object containing six integers and assigns the name data to refer to it.
print("before:", data) prints the list before any changes are made.
data[2:5] = ["a", "b"] replaces the slice of data from index 2 up to (but not including) index 5 with the two elements "a" and "b". This operation changes the existing list object in place.
print("after: ", data) prints the modified list so the effect of the slice assignment is visible.



Example 2: Nested lists and repeated references (* repetition)
List repetition with * repeats references when the repeated element is a mutable object (such as a list). The key observation is that changing one inner list changes every “row” when all rows refer to the same inner list object.
Beginner-friendly version (repeated references):

row = [0, 0]
grid_bad = [row] * 3

print("before:", grid_bad)

grid_bad[0][0] = 99

print("after: ", grid_bad)

before: [[0, 0], [0, 0], [0, 0]]
after:  [[99, 0], [99, 0], [99, 0]]


Explanation:

row = [0, 0] creates a list object containing two zeros and assigns the name row to refer to it.
grid_bad = [row] * 3 creates a new outer list with three elements, but each element is a reference to the same inner list object referenced by row. No independent inner lists are created.
print("before:", grid_bad) prints the nested list structure before mutation.
grid_bad[0][0] = 99 changes the first element of the first inner list to 99. Because all three “rows” refer to the same inner list object, the change appears in every row.
print("after: ", grid_bad) prints the nested list structure after mutation to show the repeated-reference effect.



Example 3: Nested lists created as independent inner lists (recommended construction)
A common pattern for building nested lists is to construct each inner list independently so that mutating one row does not affect the others.
Beginner-friendly version (independent inner lists):

grid_ok = [[0, 0] for _ in range(3)]

print("before:", grid_ok)

grid_ok[0][0] = 99

print("after: ", grid_ok)

before: [[0, 0], [0, 0], [0, 0]]
after:  [[99, 0], [0, 0], [0, 0]]


Explanation:

grid_ok = [[0, 0] for _ in range(3)] builds a new outer list by running the expression [0, 0] three times, producing three separate inner list objects. The name grid_ok refers to the resulting nested list.
print("before:", grid_ok) prints the nested list structure before mutation.
grid_ok[0][0] = 99 changes the first element of the first inner list to 99. Because each row is a different inner list object, the change affects only the first row.
print("after: ", grid_ok) prints the nested list structure after mutation to show that only one row changed.

title: CMSC 1236 — Week 3 Demo
section: Part 4: Dictionaries (mapping type): insertion order, safe access, and controlled updates
text:
Part 4: Dictionaries (mapping type): insertion order, safe access, and controlled updates
There are four dictionary behaviors in this unit that are important for writing predictable programs with mappings.
A dictionary is the built-in mapping type: it stores values by key rather than by position. Because dictionaries are used to represent configuration, lookup tables, and structured records, it is important to understand how key order behaves, how to access keys safely, and how to update dictionaries without accidentally changing an object that other parts of a program still rely on.
The examples that follow demonstrate:

insertion order during iteration (order of insertion, not sorting),
safe access using membership tests and get when keys may be missing,
controlled updates using copy and update to preserve an original mapping,
key-view comparisons using set operations to reason about which keys match or differ.


Example 1: Insertion order (iteration is not sorting)
This example shows that dictionaries preserve the order in which keys were inserted. The key observation is that the dictionary’s key order matches insertion order, not alphabetical order.

d = {"b": 2, "a": 1, "c": 3}

print("dictionary =", d)
print("keys in iteration order =", list(d.keys()))

dictionary = {'b': 2, 'a': 1, 'c': 3}
keys in iteration order = ['b', 'a', 'c']


Explanation:

d = {"b": 2, "a": 1, "c": 3} creates a dictionary with three key-value pairs and assigns the name d to refer to it.
print("dictionary =", d) prints the dictionary so the insertion order is visible in the displayed representation.
print("keys in iteration order =", list(d.keys())) converts the dictionary’s keys view to a list and prints it. The result reflects insertion order.



Example 2: Safe access with membership tests and get
This example shows two standard ways to handle keys that may or may not exist. The key observation is that membership tests report whether a key is present, and get returns a default value instead of raising an error when a key is missing.

config = {"mode": "test"}

print("config =", config)

print("'mode' in config =", "mode" in config)
print("'timeout' in config =", "timeout" in config)

print("config.get('mode') =", config.get("mode"))
print("config.get('timeout', 30) =", config.get("timeout", 30))

config = {'mode': 'test'}
'mode' in config = True
'timeout' in config = False
config.get('mode') = test
config.get('timeout', 30) = 30


Explanation:

config = {"mode": "test"} creates a dictionary with one key-value pair and assigns config to refer to it.
print("config =", config) prints the dictionary.
print("'mode' in config =", "mode" in config) tests whether the key "mode" is present in the dictionary.
print("'timeout' in config =", "timeout" in config) tests whether the key "timeout" is present in the dictionary.
print("config.get('mode') =", config.get("mode")) retrieves the value for "mode". Because the key exists, the value is returned.
print("config.get('timeout', 30) =", config.get("timeout", 30)) attempts to retrieve the value for "timeout". Because the key does not exist, the default value 30 is returned instead.



Example 3: Controlled updates using copy and update
This example shows how to combine a base dictionary with an override dictionary without modifying the original. The key observation is that copy creates a new dictionary object, and update mutates that new dictionary by applying key-value pairs from another dictionary.

base = {"host": "localhost", "port": 8000}
override = {"port": 9000}

merged = base.copy()
merged.update(override)

print("base =", base)
print("override =", override)
print("merged =", merged)

base = {'host': 'localhost', 'port': 8000}
override = {'port': 9000}
merged = {'host': 'localhost', 'port': 9000}


Explanation:

base = {"host": "localhost", "port": 8000} creates a dictionary containing default settings.
override = {"port": 9000} creates a second dictionary containing settings that should replace or extend defaults.
merged = base.copy() creates a new dictionary object with the same key-value pairs as base and assigns merged to refer to it.
merged.update(override) modifies merged in place by applying key-value pairs from override. In this case, the "port" key is overwritten with 9000.
The three print statements display base, override, and merged so that it is clear the original base dictionary was not changed.



Example 4: Dictionary key views for comparisons
This example shows that keys() returns a view of keys that can be used in set operations. The key observation is that intersection identifies keys present in both dictionaries, and difference identifies keys present in one dictionary but not the other.

a = {"x": 1, "y": 2, "z": 3}
b = {"y": 20, "z": 30, "w": 40}

common = a.keys() & b.keys()
only_in_a = a.keys() - b.keys()

print("a keys =", list(a.keys()))
print("b keys =", list(b.keys()))
print("keys in both =", common)
print("keys only in a =", only_in_a)

a keys = ['x', 'y', 'z']
b keys = ['y', 'z', 'w']
keys in both = {'y', 'z'}
keys only in a = {'x'}


Explanation:

a = {"x": 1, "y": 2, "z": 3} creates a dictionary with three keys and assigns a to refer to it.
b = {"y": 20, "z": 30, "w": 40} creates a second dictionary with an overlapping set of keys and assigns b to refer to it.
common = a.keys() & b.keys() computes the intersection of the two key views, producing the set of keys that appear in both dictionaries.
only_in_a = a.keys() - b.keys() computes the difference, producing the set of keys that appear in a but not in b.
The print statements display the keys and the computed comparisons.



Example 5: Dictionary comprehensions
This example shows that dictionaries can be built using a comprehension, which follows the same pattern as the list comprehensions introduced in CMSC 1203.
Dictionary comprehensions are useful when you need to transform existing data into a lookup table. A common situation is having two related lists—such as a list of names and a list of scores—where corresponding items share the same position. With two separate lists, looking up a specific person’s score requires searching through the names list to find the position, then using that position to index into the scores list. This approach is tedious to write, easy to get wrong, and becomes a problem if the two lists ever get out of sync.
A dictionary comprehension provides a concise way to build this structure in a single expression when the source data is already available in a pairable form.

names = ["alice", "bob", "carol"]
scores = [85, 92, 78]

gradebook = {name: score for name, score in zip(names, scores)}

print("names =", names)
print("scores =", scores)
print("gradebook =", gradebook)
print("gradebook['bob'] =", gradebook["bob"])

names = ['alice', 'bob', 'carol']
scores = [85, 92, 78]
gradebook = {'alice': 85, 'bob': 92, 'carol': 78}
gradebook['bob'] = 92


Explanation:

names = ["alice", "bob", "carol"] creates a list of student names.
scores = [85, 92, 78] creates a list of corresponding scores, where each position matches the same position in names.
gradebook = {name: score for name, score in zip(names, scores)} builds a dictionary by iterating over pairs of names and scores. The zip() function combines the two lists into pairs, and the comprehension creates a key-value pair for each. The result is a dictionary that maps each name to their score.
print("gradebook =", gradebook) prints the dictionary so the structure is visible.
print("gradebook['bob'] =", gradebook["bob"]) retrieves Bob’s score by name, demonstrating that the dictionary provides direct lookup without searching through a list.

title: CMSC 1236 — Week 3 Demo
section: Part 5: Set types: uniqueness and hashability
text:
Part 5: Set types: uniqueness and hashability
A set is a built-in collection type used to represent a group of unique values. Unlike lists and tuples, sets do not keep items in a positional order, and duplicate values are automatically removed.
Sets are useful when the question is “is this value present?” or when a collection needs to be reduced to distinct values. To support fast membership testing, set elements must be hashable, which requires that an element’s value not change in a way that would affect its hash. For that reason, mutable objects such as lists cannot be set elements.
The examples that follow demonstrate two points: constructing a set removes duplicates from an input sequence, and attempting to place an unhashable value (such as a list) into a set results in an error.

Example 1: De-duplication by constructing a set
This example shows that building a set from a list removes duplicate values. The key observation is that repeated items from the list appear only once in the set.

items = ["apple", "apple", "pear", "banana", "banana"]

unique_items = set(items)

print("original list =", items)
print("set (unique values) =", unique_items)

original list = ['apple', 'apple', 'pear', 'banana', 'banana']
set (unique values) = {'pear', 'apple', 'banana'}


Explanation:

items = ["apple", "apple", "pear", "banana", "banana"] creates a list that contains repeated strings and assigns the name items to refer to it.
unique_items = set(items) constructs a set from the list. Because sets store only distinct values, duplicates are removed.
print("original list =", items) prints the original list so the duplicates are visible.
print("set (unique values) =", unique_items) prints the set so the unique values are visible.



Example 2: Hashability requirement (what can be an element of a set)
This example shows that lists cannot be elements of a set. The key observation is that Python raises a TypeError because lists are mutable and therefore not hashable.

try:
    bad = {[1, 2], [3, 4]}
except TypeError as e:
    print("error:", e)

error: cannot use 'list' as a set element (unhashable type: 'list')


Explanation:

The try block attempts to create a set containing two list objects: [1, 2] and [3, 4].
Python raises a TypeError because list objects are mutable and cannot be used as set elements.
The except TypeError as e block catches the error and prints the message so the reason for the failure is visible.

title: CMSC 1236 — Week 3 Demo
section: Part 6: Tuples (sequence type, immutable): record-style grouping
text:
Part 6: Tuples (sequence type, immutable): record-style grouping
A tuple is a built-in sequence type, which means it holds an ordered collection of items that can be accessed by position (index). Tuples are similar to lists in how they are written and indexed, but they differ in one important way.
A list is mutable, meaning its contents can be changed after it is created. A tuple is immutable, meaning that after a tuple is created, the individual items inside it cannot be replaced using assignment. This makes tuples useful for grouping a fixed set of related values into a single object, where each position has a stable meaning (for example, a first name, last name, and year).
The examples that follow demonstrate two points: tuple items can be accessed by index like other sequences, and attempting to assign to a tuple element results in an error because tuples do not support item assignment.

Example 1: Accessing tuple items by index
This example shows that tuples support indexing. The key observation is that the first and last items can be accessed using 0 and -1, just as with lists.

record = ("Ada", "Lovelace", 1815)

print("record =", record)
print("first item =", record[0])
print("last item =", record[-1])

record = ('Ada', 'Lovelace', 1815)
first item = Ada
last item = 1815


Explanation:

record = ("Ada", "Lovelace", 1815) creates a tuple containing three items and assigns the name record to refer to it.
print("record =", record) prints the tuple so its contents are visible.
print("first item =", record[0]) prints the first item in the tuple (index 0).
print("last item =", record[-1]) prints the last item in the tuple (index -1 counts from the end).



Example 2: Tuples do not allow item assignment (immutability)
This example shows that tuple items cannot be reassigned. The key observation is that Python raises a TypeError when an assignment to a tuple element is attempted.

record = ("Ada", "Lovelace", 1815)

try:
    record[0] = "ADA"
except TypeError as e:
    print("error:", e)

error: 'tuple' object does not support item assignment


Explanation:

record = ("Ada", "Lovelace", 1815) creates a tuple containing three items and assigns record to refer to it.
The try block attempts to assign a new value to record[0].
Python raises a TypeError because tuples are immutable and do not support item assignment.
The except TypeError as e block catches the error and prints the message.

title: CMSC 1236 — Week 3 Demo
section: Part 7: Copying nested collection objects (shallow vs deep)
text:
Part 7: Copying nested collection objects (shallow vs deep)
When a collection contains other collection objects, the structure is nested. A nested structure is one where an outer object (such as a list) contains inner objects (such as other lists). When the inner objects are mutable, changes to an inner object can affect more than one variable if those variables share references to the same inner object.
Copying matters because programs often need a “starting version” of data and a “working version” of data at the same time. For example, a program might keep an original configuration, menu, or table of values unchanged while also building a modified version for output or for the next step in a computation. If the copy shares inner objects with the original, then changing the working version can unexpectedly change the original as well.
Two terms are used in this section:

shallow copy: creates a new outer collection, but the inner objects are shared
deep copy: creates a new outer collection and also creates copies of nested objects

The examples that follow show the practical difference by mutating an inner list and observing whether the original structure changes.

Example 1: Shallow copy shares inner lists
This example shows that slicing a nested list (nested[:]) creates a new outer list but does not copy the inner lists. The key observation is that changing an inner list through the shallow copy also changes the original.

nested = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
shallow = nested[:]

print("nested before =", nested)
print("shallow before =", shallow)

shallow[0].append(999)

print("nested after  =", nested)
print("shallow after =", shallow)
print("nested is shallow =", nested is shallow)
print("nested[0] is shallow[0] =", nested[0] is shallow[0])

nested before = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
shallow before = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
nested after  = [[1, 2, 999], [3, 4]]
shallow after = [[1, 2, 999], [3, 4]]
nested is shallow = False
nested[0] is shallow[0] = True


Explanation:

nested = [[1, 2], [3, 4]] creates an outer list that contains two inner lists, and assigns nested to refer to it.
shallow = nested[:] creates a new outer list containing the same two inner list objects, and assigns shallow to refer to it. The inner lists are not copied.
The first two print statements display nested and shallow before any changes.
shallow[0].append(999) modifies the first inner list in place by appending 999. Because nested[0] and shallow[0] refer to the same inner list object, the change appears in both nested and shallow.
The next two print statements display nested and shallow after the mutation.
print("nested is shallow =", nested is shallow) shows that the outer lists are different objects.
print("nested[0] is shallow[0] =", nested[0] is shallow[0]) shows that the first inner list object is shared.



Example 2: Deep copy creates independent nested objects
This example shows that a deep copy duplicates the nested structure. The key observation is that changing an inner list in the deep copy does not change the original.

import copy

nested = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
deep = copy.deepcopy(nested)

print("nested before =", nested)
print("deep before   =", deep)

deep[0].append(999)

print("nested after  =", nested)
print("deep after    =", deep)
print("nested is deep =", nested is deep)
print("nested[0] is deep[0] =", nested[0] is deep[0])

nested before = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
deep before   = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
nested after  = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
deep after    = [[1, 2, 999], [3, 4]]
nested is deep = False
nested[0] is deep[0] = False


Explanation:

import copy loads the copy module so that deepcopy can be used.
nested = [[1, 2], [3, 4]] creates an outer list containing two inner lists and assigns nested to refer to it.
deep = copy.deepcopy(nested) creates a new outer list and also creates new inner list objects with the same contents, then assigns deep to refer to the copied structure.
The first two print statements display nested and deep before any changes.
deep[0].append(999) modifies the first inner list in the deep copy by appending 999.
The next two print statements display nested and deep after the mutation. nested is unchanged because the inner lists are not shared.
print("nested is deep =", nested is deep) shows that the outer lists are different objects.
print("nested[0] is deep[0] =", nested[0] is deep[0]) shows that the first inner list objects are different, confirming that the nested lists were copied.

title: CMSC 1236 — Week 3 Demo
section: Part 8: File access with pathlib.Path (modern interface)
text:
Part 8: File access with pathlib.Path (modern interface)
Basic file handling using open() and a with block was introduced in CMSC 1203, and the textbook’s Chapter 9 presents that same approach as the standard, universal way to work with files in Python. This section does not re-teach that baseline.
Instead, the focus here is a modern alternative interface that is widely used in current Python code: the pathlib module. This module is not covered in CMSC 1203 and it is not a topic in Learning Python Chapters 8–9, but it is a practical tool that builds directly on the same underlying file concepts.
A pathlib.Path object represents a file path as an object rather than as a plain string. It can simplify common tasks such as reading and writing text and helps keep file-related code consistent and readable as programs grow.
The pathlib interface shown here provides a more concise alternative for common text operations: read_text() and write_text() handle opening, reading or writing, and closing in a single method call. Both approaches are valid; pathlib is often simpler when all you need is to read or write an entire file as a string.
The examples that follow demonstrate how to:

create a Path object for a file,
read an entire text file with read_text(),
write text to a file with write_text(),
and confirm the results by reading the file again.


Example 1: File access with pathlib.Path
In a notebook, files are created in the notebook’s working directory. The working directory is the folder Python treats as the “current location” when you use a relative file name like demo_text.txt. If the file name does not include a folder path, it refers to a file in that current folder.
This example writes a text file first (so the file always exists), then reads it back.

from pathlib import Path

path = Path("demo_text.txt")

path.write_text("first line\nsecond line\nthird line\n")

text = path.read_text()
print("file contents:\n" + text)

path.write_text("replacement line 1\nreplacement line 2\n")

updated_text = path.read_text()
print("updated file contents:\n" + updated_text)

file contents:
first line
second line
third line

updated file contents:
replacement line 1
replacement line 2



Explanation:

from pathlib import Path imports the Path class from the pathlib module.
path = Path("demo_text.txt") creates a Path object representing a file named demo_text.txt in the current working directory.
path.write_text("first line\nsecond line\nthird line\n") writes the given string to the file. If the file does not exist, it is created. If it already exists, its contents are replaced.
text = path.read_text() reads the entire file and returns it as a single string.
print("file contents:\n" + text) prints the contents that were read so the result is visible.
path.write_text("replacement line 1\nreplacement line 2\n") writes new text to the same file, replacing the previous contents.
updated_text = path.read_text() reads the file again after the update.
print("updated file contents:\n" + updated_text) prints the updated contents so the change is visible.

text:
Summary of key points

Names refer to objects; collection objects store references to other objects.
is tests identity; == tests value equality.
Rebinding and mutation produce different effects.
Lists and dictionaries are mutable; tuples are immutable.
Set types enforce uniqueness and require hashable elements.
Shallow copies share nested objects; deep copies replicate nested objects.
pathlib.Path provides a modern way to read and write text files.

text:
Part 2: Debugging Exercise

The Scenario
You’ve been hired as a junior developer at Sunshine Animal Shelter. On your first day, several employees come to you with complaints about the shelter management program:
Maria (Event Coordinator): > “I created a separate list for Saturday’s adoption event and added a new dog named Charlie to it. But now Charlie is showing up on the main shelter roster too! I didn’t add him there. The lists are supposed to be separate.”
James (Feeding Staff): > “I updated Wednesday’s evening feeding to 10 portions for a special diet. But now EVERY day shows 10 portions in the evening! I only changed Wednesday!”
Dr. Patel (Veterinarian): > “Before transferring Whiskers to a partner shelter, I made a backup of her profile. Then I added a transfer exam note to the backup. But somehow that note appeared on her original record too! The backup was supposed to be independent.”
Sarah (Adoption Specialist): > “I processed three adoptions today — Hoppy, Max, and Luna. But when I looked at the records, they all have each other’s information mixed together! Hoppy’s record shows Max’s adopter name, and Luna’s record has everyone’s notes. Each adoption should have its own separate paperwork.”
Tom (Database Admin): > “We have two entries for a dog named Bella — same name, same breed, same age. The duplicate checker says they’re different pets, but they’re clearly the same dog entered twice. Why isn’t it catching this?”
Your supervisor says: > “The weird thing is, the program runs fine — no error messages. But the output is clearly wrong. These are all experienced employees; they know how the system should work. Can you figure out what’s going on in the code?”


About the Program
This program simulates a full day of shelter operations automatically — there’s no user input or menu system. When you run it, it immediately executes all six sections and prints the results. This is by design, so you can focus on reading the output and finding bugs rather than navigating menus.
The program is also longer than what you’ve written so far, and it uses a few Python features we haven’t covered yet. These features are only used in the display/helper functions — not in the buggy functions you need to fix. You don’t need to fully understand them to complete this assignment, but here’s what they do:







Code
What It Does




enumerate(pets, 1)
Loops through a list while also tracking a counter that starts at 1 (used for numbered lists)


f"{text:^50}"
Centers text within 50 characters (used for headers)


f"{text:<12}"
Left-aligns text within 12 characters (used for table columns)



Focus on the five short functions where the bugs live — each is only 2–5 lines of straightforward code using concepts from Chapters 8–9. The five functions you need to debug are:

copy_available_pets()
create_feeding_schedule()
backup_pet_profile()
register_adoption()
find_duplicate_profiles()

The rest of the program (display functions, sample data setup, the Pet class, main()) is scaffolding that just works.
Important: Your fixes should only involve changing code within these five functions. Do not add new functions, delete existing functions, or modify the main() automation. Each bug can be fixed by changing 1–2 lines in its function.


Your Task
The program contains 5 bugs — one causing each employee’s problem. Each bug relates to a concept from Chapters 8–9.

Download the program using the link above
Rename the file to include your last name (smith-pet-shelter.py)
Run the program and observe the output
Investigate each employee’s complaint by finding the responsible function
For each bug:

Identify the function and line where the bug occurs
Explain the concept being violated
Fix the code

Run the corrected program to verify you’ve resolved all five complaints



How to Find the Bugs

Read the output carefully. Compare what you see to each employee’s complaint.
Trace the code. When you see unexpected output, find the function responsible and read it line by line.
Think about Chapters 8–9. Each bug involves one of these concepts: copying lists, creating nested lists, copying objects with nested data, function parameters, or comparing objects.

title: Week 5 Assignments
text:
Part 1: Reading
Read Learning Python (6e), Chapters 10–11.
As you read, focus on these ideas (these are also the ideas the quiz will test):

Program hierarchy: programs are composed of modules, modules contain statements, statements contain expressions, and expressions create and process objects
Statements vs expressions: statements perform actions; expressions produce values — and each can appear only in certain places
Indentation as syntax: Python uses indentation to define blocks, the colon signals the start of an indented block, and inconsistent indentation causes an IndentationError
Line continuation: how to continue a long statement across lines using backslash or open brackets, and how to place multiple statements on one line with semicolons
Extended sequence unpacking: the * operator collects remaining elements into a list during unpacking (e.g., first, *rest = [1, 2, 3, 4])
Augmented assignment: operators like +=, -=, and //=, and how += can modify a mutable object in place rather than creating a new one
Multi-target assignment: a = b = c = 0 creates a shared reference, and mutations through one name affect all names
Named assignment expressions (walrus operator): := assigns a value and returns it in a single expression, useful in conditions and loops
Expression statements: expressions like my_list.sort() can stand alone as statements when called for their side effects
Print parameters: sep, end, file, and flush control how print() formats and delivers output

title: Week 5 Demo
text:
CMSC 1203 introduced Python’s core syntax: variables, conditionals, loops, functions, and data structures. You learned to write correct Python code by following rules about indentation, colons, and assignment. Chapters 10–11 of Learning Python build on that foundation by explaining why Python works the way it does and introducing assignment features that go beyond what Gaddis covered.
This unit shifts focus to Python’s design decisions. The goal is to understand how Python organizes code internally, why the language uses indentation instead of braces, and what distinguishes a statement from an expression. These concepts explain behavior that may have seemed arbitrary in CMSC 1203 and prepare you for more advanced Python features.
After completing these examples, you should be able to:

describe Python’s program hierarchy (programs, modules, statements, expressions, objects) and use introspection to observe it
explain the difference between statements and expressions, and identify where each can appear in code
explain why Python uses indentation to define blocks rather than braces or keywords
use extended sequence unpacking (*) to capture variable-length portions of sequences
use named assignment expressions (:=) to assign and use a value in a single expression
use the print() function’s advanced parameters (sep, end, file, flush) to control output formatting

The examples in this demo are organized into four parts. Part 1 establishes the conceptual foundation that explains Python’s structure. Parts 2–4 introduce specific syntax features that build on that foundation.

title: Week 5 Demo
text:
Part 1: Python’s Building Blocks
Python code is organized into a hierarchy of components. Understanding this hierarchy helps you predict how code behaves and debug problems when they occur. It also explains why certain syntax rules exist and why some code works in certain places but not others.
The hierarchy has four levels:

Programs are composed of modules
Modules contain statements
Statements contain expressions
Expressions create and process objects

At the bottom of this hierarchy are objects—the actual values your program works with (integers, strings, lists, dictionaries, etc.). Everything in Python is an object, and every piece of code you write either creates objects, processes objects, or both.
This part demonstrates each level of the hierarchy using Python’s introspection capabilities, then examines the critical distinction between statements and expressions.

1.1 The Program Hierarchy
A Python program consists of one or more modules. A module is simply a .py file containing Python code. When you write a script or import a library, you are working with modules.

Example 1: Modules are objects
Python treats modules as objects that can be inspected. This example shows that an imported module has a type and contains names defined by statements in that module.

import math

print("type(math) =", type(math))
print()
print("First 10 names in math module:")
print(dir(math)[:10])

type(math) = <class 'module'>

First 10 names in math module:
['__doc__', '__file__', '__loader__', '__name__', '__package__', '__spec__', 'acos', 'acosh', 'asin', 'asinh']


Explanation:

import math loads the math module and makes it available under the name math. The import statement is how Python brings module objects into your program.
print("type(math) =", type(math)) displays the type of the math object. The output <class 'module'> confirms that modules are objects with their own type.
print(dir(math)[:10]) displays the first 10 names defined in the math module. The dir() function returns a list of names (functions, constants, etc.) that statements in the module created.

The key insight is that a module is an object containing other objects. The statements inside math.py (assignment statements, function definitions, etc.) created the names you see in dir(math).


Example 2: Tracing the hierarchy in your own code
Every line of code you write fits somewhere in the hierarchy. This example traces a single line through all four levels.

x = 3 + 4

print("x =", x)
print("type(x) =", type(x))
print("id(x) =", id(x))

x = 7
type(x) = <class 'int'>
id(x) = 4389250008


Explanation:

x = 3 + 4 is a statement (specifically, an assignment statement). It performs an action: binding the name x to a value.
The right side, 3 + 4, is an expression. It produces a value by combining two objects using the + operator.
The integers 3 and 4 are objects. The expression evaluates to produce another object: the integer 7.
print("x =", x) displays the value that x refers to: 7.
print("type(x) =", type(x)) displays <class 'int'>, confirming that x refers to an integer object.
print("id(x) =", id(x)) displays the object’s unique identity—its address in memory. This confirms that x refers to a specific object, not just an abstract value.

One line of code involves all four levels: the assignment is a statement, it contains an expression (3 + 4), and that expression creates and processes objects.


Example 3: Expressions evaluate to objects
Expressions can appear anywhere Python expects a value. This example shows several expressions and confirms that each evaluates to an object.

print("3 + 4 evaluates to:", 3 + 4)
print("type(3 + 4) =", type(3 + 4))
print()

print("len('hello') evaluates to:", len("hello"))
print("type(len('hello')) =", type(len("hello")))
print()

print("'hello'.upper() evaluates to:", "hello".upper())
print("type('hello'.upper()) =", type("hello".upper()))

3 + 4 evaluates to: 7
type(3 + 4) = <class 'int'>

len('hello') evaluates to: 5
type(len('hello')) = <class 'int'>

'hello'.upper() evaluates to: HELLO
type('hello'.upper()) = <class 'str'>


Explanation:

3 + 4 is an arithmetic expression that evaluates to the integer 7.
len("hello") is a function call expression that evaluates to the integer 5.
"hello".upper() is a method call expression that evaluates to the string "HELLO".

Each expression produces an object. The type() calls confirm this by showing the class of each resulting object. Because expressions produce objects, they can be used as arguments to functions, combined with other expressions, or assigned to names.



1.2 Statements vs Expressions
The distinction between statements and expressions is fundamental to understanding Python syntax. This distinction determines where certain code can and cannot appear.
Expressions produce values. An expression evaluates to an object that can be used, stored, or passed to a function.
Statements perform actions. A statement does something (assigns a name, imports a module, defines a function) but does not produce a value that can be used elsewhere.
This difference has practical consequences: expressions can appear anywhere a value is expected, but statements cannot.

Example 4: Expressions can appear where values are expected
Expressions are flexible because they produce values. This example shows expressions used in several contexts.

numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

total = sum(numbers)
print("sum(numbers) =", total)

average = sum(numbers) / len(numbers)
print("average =", average)

is_positive = total > 0
print("is_positive =", is_positive)

doubled = [x * 2 for x in numbers]
print("doubled =", doubled)

sum(numbers) = 15
average = 3.0
is_positive = True
doubled = [2, 4, 6, 8, 10]


Explanation:

sum(numbers) is an expression that evaluates to 15. It appears on the right side of an assignment.
sum(numbers) / len(numbers) combines two function call expressions with the / operator. The entire thing is an expression that evaluates to 3.0.
total > 0 is a comparison expression that evaluates to True or False.
x * 2 is an expression inside a list comprehension. It evaluates once for each element.

In each case, the expression produces a value that gets used: assigned to a name, passed to print(), or collected into a list.


Example 5: Statements cannot appear where values are expected
Statements perform actions but do not produce values. This example shows what happens when you try to use a statement where an expression is required.

# This code demonstrates the error - do not run it
# Instead, observe what kind of error Python reports

# Trying to use assignment (a statement) as an argument:
# print(x = 10)

# The error message would be:
# TypeError: 'x' is an invalid keyword argument for print()

# Python interprets x=10 as a keyword argument, not assignment.
# If we try to use assignment in a context where it clearly 
# can't be a keyword argument, we get a syntax error.

# For example, this would cause a syntax error:
# result = (x = 10) + 5
# SyntaxError: invalid syntax

Explanation:

Assignment (x = 10) is a statement. It binds the name x to the value 10, but it does not produce a value itself.
When you write print(x = 10), Python does not see an assignment statement. It interprets x=10 as a keyword argument (because print() accepts keyword arguments like sep= and end=).
In contexts where keyword argument interpretation is not possible, using = where an expression is expected causes a SyntaxError.

The key insight is that statements and expressions serve different purposes. Statements are for performing actions; expressions are for producing values. Python’s syntax keeps these roles separate.


Example 6: Why the distinction matters
Understanding statements vs expressions helps you read Python code and explains why certain patterns exist. This example shows a common situation where you might want to assign and use a value in the same place.

# Common pattern: check a condition and use the result

data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

# Approach 1: Compute twice (wasteful if computation is expensive)
if len(data) > 0:
    print("Length is:", len(data))

# Approach 2: Compute once, store, then check
length = len(data)
if length > 0:
    print("Length is:", length)

# What if you could assign AND check in one place?
# This is exactly what the walrus operator (:=) provides.
# We will cover := in Part 3.

Length is: 5
Length is: 5


Explanation:

In Approach 1, len(data) is called twice: once in the condition and once in the body. For a simple operation like len(), this is fine. For expensive operations, it wastes time.
In Approach 2, the result is stored in length first, then used twice. This requires an extra line and an extra name.
The walrus operator (:=) solves this by making assignment an expression—you can assign a value and use it in the same place. Part 3 covers this in detail.

The statement/expression distinction is why the walrus operator was added to Python 3.8. It fills a gap that the traditional assignment statement cannot fill.



1.3 Why Python Uses Indentation
You already write indented Python code because you learned to do so in CMSC 1203. This section explains why Python uses indentation as syntax, which helps you understand the language’s design and appreciate what it prevents.

The design decision
Many programming languages use symbols to mark where blocks of code begin and end. Some languages use curly braces { }. Others use keywords like BEGIN and END. In those languages, indentation is optional—it helps humans read the code, but the computer ignores it and uses only the symbols.
This creates a potential problem: the code’s appearance can disagree with its meaning. A programmer might indent code to show structure, but if the braces are wrong, the computer sees different structure. The code looks like it does one thing but does another.
Python made a different choice: indentation is the syntax. The whitespace you use to organize your code is how Python determines block structure. There are no braces, no BEGIN/END—just indentation.
This means your code’s appearance always matches its meaning. What you see is what Python sees.


What Python adds: the colon
Python’s compound statements (statements that contain other statements) follow a consistent pattern:
header line:
    indented block
    more statements in block
The colon (:) signals that an indented block follows. Every compound statement you know uses this pattern.


Example 7: The compound statement pattern
This example shows the consistent structure across different compound statements.

# if statement
x = 10
if x > 0:
    print("x is positive")
    print("this is still in the if block")
print("this is outside the if block")

print()

# for loop
for i in range(3):
    print("i =", i)
    print("this is still in the loop")
print("this is outside the loop")

print()

# function definition
def greet(name):
    message = "Hello, " + name
    print(message)

greet("Alice")

x is positive
this is still in the if block
this is outside the if block

i = 0
this is still in the loop
i = 1
this is still in the loop
i = 2
this is still in the loop
this is outside the loop

Hello, Alice


Explanation:

The if statement header ends with a colon. The two print() calls are indented, so they belong to the if block. The third print() is not indented, so it runs regardless of the condition.
The for loop follows the same pattern: header with colon, indented body.
The function definition also follows this pattern: def greet(name): is the header, and the indented lines are the function body.

Once you recognize this pattern, every compound statement in Python follows the same structure. When you learn new statements like try/except, with, or match/case, they all use header-colon-indent.


Example 8: Indentation defines the block
The level of indentation determines which statements belong to which block. This example shows how changing indentation changes program behavior.

# Version 1: both prints are inside the if
print("Version 1:")
x = 5
if x > 0:
    print("  x is positive")
    print("  done checking x")
print("  program continues")

print()

# Version 2: second print is outside the if
print("Version 2:")
x = -5
if x > 0:
    print("  x is positive")
print("  done checking x")  # This runs regardless of x
print("  program continues")

Version 1:
  x is positive
  done checking x
  program continues

Version 2:
  done checking x
  program continues


Explanation:

In Version 1, both “x is positive” and “done checking x” are indented under the if. They only run when x > 0.
In Version 2, “done checking x” is not indented under the if. It runs regardless of the condition.
The behavior difference comes entirely from indentation. There are no braces to get wrong—the visual structure is the logical structure.



Example 9: Python enforces consistent indentation
If statements within the same block have inconsistent indentation, Python raises an error. This prevents the appearance/meaning mismatch that can occur in other languages.

# This code demonstrates the error structure
# The actual error would prevent execution

# Correct: consistent indentation
if True:
    a = 1
    b = 2  # Same indentation as previous line

# The following would cause IndentationError:
# if True:
#     a = 1
#    b = 2  # IndentationError: unindent does not match

print("Python requires consistent indentation within a block")

Python requires consistent indentation within a block


Explanation:

Within a single block, all statements must have the same indentation.
If indentation is inconsistent, Python raises IndentationError before the code runs.
This enforcement ensures that the visual structure of your code always matches Python’s interpretation.

The benefit is that you cannot accidentally have code that looks structured but is not. The indentation you write for readability is the same indentation Python uses for execution.

title: Week 5 Demo
text:
Part 2: Extended Sequence Unpacking
In CMSC 1203, you learned basic sequence unpacking: assigning multiple values from a sequence to multiple names in a single statement.

point = (3, 4)
x, y = point  # x = 3, y = 4

This works when you know exactly how many elements the sequence contains. But what if the sequence has a variable number of elements, or you only care about certain positions?
Python’s extended unpacking syntax uses * to capture “the rest” of a sequence into a list. This feature was added in Python 3 and is not covered in Gaddis.

2.1 The * Operator in Unpacking
The * operator, when used before a name in an unpacking assignment, collects any “leftover” elements into a list.

Example 10: First and rest pattern
A common need is to separate the first element from everything else. The * operator makes this simple.

numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

first, *rest = numbers

print("numbers =", numbers)
print("first =", first)
print("rest =", rest)
print("type(rest) =", type(rest))

numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
first = 1
rest = [2, 3, 4, 5]
type(rest) = <class 'list'>


Explanation:

first, *rest = numbers unpacks the list. The name first receives the first element (1).
The *rest collects all remaining elements into a new list: [2, 3, 4, 5].
The result of * is always a list, even if it collects zero or one element.

This pattern is useful when you need to process the first item differently from the rest.


Example 11: Rest and last pattern
You can also place * at the beginning to collect everything except the last element.

numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

*init, last = numbers

print("numbers =", numbers)
print("init =", init)
print("last =", last)

numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
init = [1, 2, 3, 4]
last = 5


Explanation:

*init, last = numbers unpacks the list. The name last receives the final element (5).
The *init collects all preceding elements into a list: [1, 2, 3, 4].

This pattern is useful when the last element has special meaning, such as a total or summary value.


Example 12: First, middle, and last pattern
You can use * in the middle to capture everything between the first and last elements.

numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

first, *middle, last = numbers

print("numbers =", numbers)
print("first =", first)
print("middle =", middle)
print("last =", last)

numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
first = 1
middle = [2, 3, 4]
last = 5


Explanation:

first, *middle, last = numbers assigns first = 1, last = 5, and middle = [2, 3, 4].
The *middle collects whatever is left after assigning the first and last positions.

This pattern is useful when sequences have a header, body, and footer structure.


Example 13: The starred name can be empty
If the sequence has exactly enough elements for the non-starred names, the starred name receives an empty list.

pair = [1, 2]

first, *middle, last = pair

print("pair =", pair)
print("first =", first)
print("middle =", middle)
print("last =", last)

pair = [1, 2]
first = 1
middle = []
last = 2


Explanation:

pair has exactly two elements. first gets 1, last gets 2.
*middle collects whatever is left: nothing. It becomes an empty list [].

This is not an error. The * name simply collects zero elements when none remain.



2.2 Practical Applications
Extended unpacking is particularly useful in loops that process records with variable-length data.

Example 14: Processing records with variable scores
Suppose you have student records where each student has a name, a variable number of homework scores, and a final exam score. Extended unpacking handles this cleanly.

# Each record: (name, hw1, hw2, ..., final_exam)
records = [
    ("Alice", 85, 90, 88, 92),
    ("Bob", 78, 82, 95),
    ("Carol", 90, 85, 88, 91, 87),
]

print("Student grade summaries:")
print()

for record in records:
    name, *homework, final = record
    hw_avg = sum(homework) / len(homework) if homework else 0
    print(f"{name}:")
    print(f"  Homework scores: {homework}")
    print(f"  Homework average: {hw_avg:.1f}")
    print(f"  Final exam: {final}")
    print()

Student grade summaries:

Alice:
  Homework scores: [85, 90, 88]
  Homework average: 87.7
  Final exam: 92

Bob:
  Homework scores: [78, 82]
  Homework average: 80.0
  Final exam: 95

Carol:
  Homework scores: [90, 85, 88, 91]
  Homework average: 88.5
  Final exam: 87



Explanation:

Each record has a different number of homework scores, but all have a name at the start and a final exam at the end.
name, *homework, final = record handles any record length. The *homework collects all middle elements.
The loop processes each student uniformly despite the varying data lengths.



Example 15: Splitting head and tail for recursive thinking
Extended unpacking supports patterns common in functional programming and recursive algorithms.

def print_elements(items, depth=0):
    """Print each element with increasing indentation."""
    if not items:
        return
    
    first, *rest = items
    indent = "  " * depth
    print(f"{indent}{first}")
    print_elements(rest, depth + 1)

print("Recursive-style processing:")
print_elements(["a", "b", "c", "d"])

Recursive-style processing:
a
  b
    c
      d


Explanation:

first, *rest = items separates the first element from the rest.
The function processes first, then calls itself with rest.
This “head and tail” pattern is natural for certain algorithms and data structures.

title: Week 5 Demo
text:
Part 3: Named Assignment Expressions (Walrus Operator)
In Part 1, you learned that assignment (=) is a statement, not an expression. Statements perform actions but do not produce values. This means you cannot assign a value and use it in the same place.
Python 3.8 introduced the walrus operator (:=), which creates a named assignment expression. Unlike regular assignment, := both assigns a value to a name AND returns that value so it can be used immediately.
The operator is called the “walrus operator” because := resembles a walrus’s eyes and tusks when viewed sideways.

3.1 Basic Syntax and Purpose
The walrus operator allows you to assign a value to a name within an expression. The syntax is:
(name := expression)
The parentheses are often required due to operator precedence, and using them consistently is good practice.

Example 16: Assign and use in one expression
This example shows the basic behavior of :=.

# Without walrus: assign first, then use
length = len("hello")
print("Length is:", length)

print()

# With walrus: assign and use in one expression
print("Length is:", (n := len("hello")))
print("n is still available:", n)

Length is: 5

Length is: 5
n is still available: 5


Explanation:

The traditional approach requires two separate steps: assign, then use.
With :=, the assignment happens inside the print() call. The expression (n := len("hello")) assigns 5 to n AND evaluates to 5.
The name n remains available after the expression, just like a regular assignment.




3.2 Avoiding Redundant Computation
A primary use of := is to compute a value once, check it, and use it—without calling the function twice or adding extra lines.

Example 17: Compute once, use twice

data = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]

# Without walrus: call len() twice
if len(data) > 3:
    print(f"Data has {len(data)} elements (more than 3)")

print()

# With walrus: call len() once
if (n := len(data)) > 3:
    print(f"Data has {n} elements (more than 3)")

Data has 5 elements (more than 3)

Data has 5 elements (more than 3)


Explanation:

Without :=, the code calls len(data) twice. For len(), this is not expensive, but for slow operations it wastes time.
With :=, the length is computed once, stored in n, and the value is immediately compared to 3.
Inside the if body, n is available without recomputing.



Example 18: Filtering with an expensive operation
When processing data, you often need to transform a value, check if the result is valid, and keep the result. The walrus operator handles this cleanly.

def slow_transform(x):
    """Simulate an expensive computation."""
    print(f"  (computing transform of {x})")
    return x * 2 if x > 0 else None

data = [5, -3, 10, 0, 7, -1]

print("Without walrus (wasteful - calls function twice):")
results_wasteful = []
for x in data:
    if slow_transform(x) is not None:
        results_wasteful.append(slow_transform(x))
print("Results:", results_wasteful)

print()

print("With walrus (efficient - calls function once):")
results_efficient = []
for x in data:
    if (result := slow_transform(x)) is not None:
        results_efficient.append(result)
print("Results:", results_efficient)

Without walrus (wasteful - calls function twice):
  (computing transform of 5)
  (computing transform of 5)
  (computing transform of -3)
  (computing transform of 10)
  (computing transform of 10)
  (computing transform of 0)
  (computing transform of 7)
  (computing transform of 7)
  (computing transform of -1)
Results: [10, 20, 14]

With walrus (efficient - calls function once):
  (computing transform of 5)
  (computing transform of -3)
  (computing transform of 10)
  (computing transform of 0)
  (computing transform of 7)
  (computing transform of -1)
Results: [10, 20, 14]


Explanation:

Without :=, slow_transform(x) is called twice for each valid element: once to check, once to use.
With :=, the result is computed once, stored in result, checked, and used if valid.
The “computing transform” messages show how many times the function is actually called.




3.3 While Loop Patterns
The walrus operator is particularly useful in while loops that need to read input and check a condition.

Example 19: Input validation loop

# Simulated input for demonstration (would use input() in real code)
simulated_inputs = iter(["", "abc", "42", "done"])

def get_input(prompt):
    """Simulate user input from a predefined sequence."""
    value = next(simulated_inputs)
    print(f"{prompt}{value}")
    return value

# Without walrus: awkward structure
print("Without walrus:")
response = get_input("Enter a number (or 'done'): ")
while response != "done":
    if response.isdigit():
        print(f"  You entered the number {response}")
    else:
        print(f"  '{response}' is not a valid number")
    response = get_input("Enter a number (or 'done'): ")
print()

# Reset simulated input
simulated_inputs = iter(["", "abc", "42", "done"])

# With walrus: cleaner structure
print("With walrus:")
while (response := get_input("Enter a number (or 'done'): ")) != "done":
    if response.isdigit():
        print(f"  You entered the number {response}")
    else:
        print(f"  '{response}' is not a valid number")

Without walrus:
Enter a number (or 'done'): 
  '' is not a valid number
Enter a number (or 'done'): abc
  'abc' is not a valid number
Enter a number (or 'done'): 42
  You entered the number 42
Enter a number (or 'done'): done

With walrus:
Enter a number (or 'done'): 
  '' is not a valid number
Enter a number (or 'done'): abc
  'abc' is not a valid number
Enter a number (or 'done'): 42
  You entered the number 42
Enter a number (or 'done'): done


Explanation:

Without :=, the input call appears twice: once before the loop and once at the end of each iteration. This pattern is error-prone if you change one call but not the other.
With :=, the input call appears once in the while condition. It assigns the result to response and checks it in the same expression.
The loop body can use response directly.




3.4 List Comprehension Filtering
The walrus operator allows efficient filtering in comprehensions when the filter condition involves a computation you also need in the result.

Example 20: Transform and filter in one pass

def process(x):
    """Return transformed value, or None if invalid."""
    return x.strip().upper() if x.strip() else None

items = ["  hello  ", "", "  world  ", "   ", "python"]

# Without walrus: process each item twice
result_wasteful = [process(item) for item in items if process(item) is not None]
print("Without walrus:", result_wasteful)

# With walrus: process each item once
result_efficient = [result for item in items if (result := process(item)) is not None]
print("With walrus:", result_efficient)

Without walrus: ['HELLO', 'WORLD', 'PYTHON']
With walrus: ['HELLO', 'WORLD', 'PYTHON']


Explanation:

Without :=, process(item) is called twice for each item that passes the filter: once in the if clause and once for the result.
With :=, process(item) is called once. The result is assigned to result in the if clause and used directly as the output.
For expensive operations or large datasets, this efficiency matters.




3.5 The Parentheses Requirement
The walrus operator has lower precedence than most operators, so parentheses are often required to get the intended behavior.

Example 21: Parentheses matter

# With parentheses: assignment happens first, then comparison
if (x := len("hello")) > 3:
    print(f"Length {x} is greater than 3")

# Safe pattern: always use parentheses around :=
value = (y := 10) + 5
print(f"y = {y}, value = {value}")

# What the parentheses prevent:
# Without parentheses, := binds very loosely.
# The expression: x := len("hello") > 3
# would be parsed as: x := (len("hello") > 3)
# So x would be True (a boolean), not 5 (the length).

Length 5 is greater than 3
y = 10, value = 15


Explanation:

(x := len("hello")) assigns 5 to x AND evaluates to 5. Then 5 > 3 is evaluated.
(y := 10) + 5 assigns 10 to y AND evaluates to 10. Then 10 + 5 produces 15.
Without parentheses, := has lower precedence than most operators. The expression x := len("hello") > 3 would assign the boolean result (True) to x, not the length.
The safe practice is to always wrap := expressions in parentheses when they appear within larger expressions.

title: Week 5 Demo
text:
Part 4: Print Function Advanced Features
The print() function was introduced in CMSC 1203 for basic output. This section covers its optional parameters that control formatting, which were likely not emphasized in Gaddis.
The full signature of print() is:
print(*objects, sep=' ', end='\n', file=sys.stdout, flush=False)
The four keyword parameters control how output is formatted and where it goes.

4.1 The sep Parameter
By default, print() separates multiple arguments with a single space. The sep parameter lets you change this separator.

Example 22: Custom separators

# Default behavior: space separator
print("apple", "banana", "cherry")

# Custom separator: comma and space
print("apple", "banana", "cherry", sep=", ")

# Custom separator: newline (each item on its own line)
print("apple", "banana", "cherry", sep="\n")

# Custom separator: nothing (items run together)
print("apple", "banana", "cherry", sep="")

# Practical use: creating a formatted list
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
print("Fruits:", *fruits, sep=" | ")

apple banana cherry
apple, banana, cherry
apple
banana
cherry
applebananacherry
Fruits: | apple | banana | cherry


Explanation:

The default sep=' ' puts a space between each argument.
sep=", " creates comma-separated output.
sep="\n" puts each argument on a new line.
sep="" joins arguments with no separator.
The *fruits syntax unpacks the list into separate arguments, which sep then joins.




4.2 The end Parameter
By default, print() adds a newline (\n) after the output. The end parameter lets you change what appears at the end.

Example 23: Controlling line endings

# Default behavior: each print ends with newline
print("Line 1")
print("Line 2")
print("Line 3")

print()

# Custom end: no newline (outputs run together on same line)
print("A", end="")
print("B", end="")
print("C", end="")
print()  # Now add a newline

print()

# Custom end: custom suffix
print("Loading", end="...")
print("Done!")

print()

# Practical use: progress indicator
import time
print("Processing: ", end="")
for i in range(5):
    print(f"{i+1}", end=" ")
    # time.sleep(0.5)  # Uncomment to see the delay
print("Complete!")

Line 1
Line 2
Line 3

ABC

Loading...Done!

Processing: 1 2 3 4 5 Complete!


Explanation:

The default end='\n' causes each print() to end with a newline.
end="" suppresses the newline, so the next print() continues on the same line.
end="..." adds a custom suffix instead of a newline.
This is useful for progress indicators or building output incrementally.




4.3 The file Parameter
By default, print() writes to sys.stdout (standard output, which appears in your console). The file parameter lets you redirect output to any file-like object.

Example 24: Printing to a file

# Writing to a file using print()
with open("output.txt", "w") as f:
    print("Line 1 of output", file=f)
    print("Line 2 of output", file=f)
    print("Line 3 of output", file=f)

# Verify by reading the file
with open("output.txt", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()

print("File contents:")
print(content)

# Clean up
import os
os.remove("output.txt")

File contents:
Line 1 of output
Line 2 of output
Line 3 of output



Explanation:

file=f redirects print() output to the file object f instead of the screen.
Each print() call writes a line to the file, including the default newline.
This can be more convenient than using f.write() when you want automatic newlines and the ability to print multiple arguments.




4.4 The flush Parameter
Output is normally buffered, meaning Python may wait before actually writing to the destination. The flush parameter forces immediate output.

Example 25: Forcing immediate output

import sys

# Normal buffered output (may not appear immediately in some contexts)
print("Starting process...")

# Flushed output (appears immediately)
print("Starting process (flushed)...", flush=True)

# Practical use: progress updates in long-running operations
print("Working: ", end="", flush=True)
for i in range(5):
    print(".", end="", flush=True)
    # time.sleep(0.5)  # Uncomment to see the effect
print(" Done!")

Starting process...
Starting process (flushed)...
Working: ..... Done!


Explanation:

Buffering means output may be held temporarily for efficiency. This is usually fine, but progress indicators may not appear until the buffer is flushed.
flush=True forces Python to send the output immediately.
This is most useful when combining end="" (no newline) with real-time feedback.

title: Week 5 Demo
text:
Conclusion
This demo introduced Python’s structural concepts and several assignment features not covered in CMSC 1203.

What You’ve Learned
Python’s Building Blocks (Part 1): - Programs are organized as modules → statements → expressions → objects - You can use introspection (type(), dir(), id()) to observe this hierarchy - Statements perform actions; expressions produce values - Python uses indentation as syntax, ensuring code appearance matches behavior
Extended Sequence Unpacking (Part 2): - The * operator collects “remaining” elements into a list - Patterns: first/rest (first, *rest), rest/last (*init, last), first/middle/last (first, *middle, last) - Useful for processing variable-length records and recursive patterns
Named Assignment Expressions (Part 3): - The walrus operator (:=) makes assignment an expression - Enables assigning and using a value in one place - Common uses: avoiding redundant computation, while loop conditions, comprehension filtering - Always use parentheses around := in larger expressions
Print Function Features (Part 4): - sep controls the separator between arguments - end controls what appears after all arguments (default is newline) - file redirects output to a file or other destination - flush forces immediate output


What the Textbook Covers in More Depth
Chapters 10–11 of Learning Python provide additional detail on these topics:
Statement Syntax: - Complete rules for line continuation and multi-line statements - All compound statement forms in detail - Special cases and edge cases for indentation
Assignment Forms: - Augmented assignment (+=, -=, etc.) and how it differs from regular assignment - Multi-target assignment and its behavior with mutable objects - Sequence assignment variations beyond what this demo covered
Expression Statements: - When expressions can stand alone as statements - The role of expression statements in Python’s execution model
Print Function: - The relationship between print() and sys.stdout - Stream redirection patterns - Differences from Python 2’s print statement (historical context)

title: Week 7 Demo
text:
CMSC 1203 taught you to write decision structures and loops. You know how to use if/elif/else, while, for, break, and continue to control program flow. Chapters 12–13 of Learning Python revisit these tools to explain design decisions you may not have encountered before and introduce features that go beyond what Gaddis covered.
This unit has two main threads. The first is how Python evaluates truth, not just True and False, but how every object carries its own truth value and how the Boolean operators and and or use that fact in ways that may surprise you. The second is how the for loop interacts with Python’s reference model, the same model you studied in Weeks 3–4. Understanding this connection explains behaviors that look like bugs but are actually consistent with how Python handles names and objects.
Along the way, you’ll also meet the match/case statement (new in Python 3.10), the loop else clause and the problem it was designed to solve, and the built-in functions zip and enumerate that make for loops more powerful without adding complexity.
After completing these examples, you should be able to:

explain why and and or return operand objects rather than True or False, and use bool() to observe truth values
use short-circuit evaluation intentionally, including for default values, guarded access, and understanding when expressions are skipped
write a basic match/case statement for multiple-choice selection
explain what the loop else clause does, how break interacts with it, and what search problem it solves
explain why modifying a for loop variable does not change the list being iterated, using the reference model from Weeks 3–4
use zip() for parallel traversal, enumerate() for offset+item access, and tuple unpacking in for loop targets

The examples are organized into five parts. Part 1 examines truth value testing and Boolean operator behavior. Part 2 introduces the match/case statement. Part 3 explains the loop else clause. Part 4, the centerpiece, connects for loop mechanics to the reference model. Part 5 covers zip, enumerate, and unpacking techniques in loops.
